# Locality Guide: Alabama Region, Pre-Statehood (c. 1780–1819)

*(Guide saved to `alabama-pre-statehood-locality-guide.md`)*

---

## The most important thing to understand first

**"Alabama" as a political entity did not exist in 1800.** Your ancestor's records were created by whoever actually governed that location at that date — and around 1800, that could be three entirely different governments depending on which part of the region they lived in. Research must be organized in layers:

| Location | Authority c. 1800 | Records created by |
|----------|------------------|--------------------|
| Northern/central interior | Mississippi Territory (U.S., 1798–1817) | Territorial courts, tax lists, militia rolls |
| Mobile Bay / southern strip | Spain (until U.S. seizure in 1813) | Spanish colonial registers, land grants |
| Anywhere after 1817 | Alabama Territory → State (1819) | County-level records begin in earnest |

---

## Boundary and jurisdictional timeline

| Period | Authority | Key records produced |
|--------|-----------|----------------------|
| 1702–1763 | **France** (Province of Louisiana) | Colonial censuses, church registers, land grants — Mobile area |
| 1763–1780 | **Britain** (West Florida colony) | Land grants, court records, loyalist claims |
| 1780–1813 | **Spain** (District of Mobile) | Census returns (padrones), church registers, land grants |
| 1798–1817 | **Mississippi Territory** (U.S.) | Territorial censuses, tax lists, militia rolls, court records, land entries |
| 1800 | Washington County formed | First U.S. county in present-day Alabama |
| 1808–1813 | Madison, Baldwin, Clarke Counties formed | Record-keeping begins county by county |
| 1817–1819 | **Alabama Territory** | Transition to statehood |
| Dec. 1819 | **State of Alabama** | County records continue and expand |

> For an ancestor present in 1800, most of northern and central Alabama had **no organized U.S. county yet**. They appear only in territorial tax lists or militia musters — if at all.

---

## Available record types

### Vital records (civil registration)
- **Statewide registration did not begin until 1908.** Before that, births and deaths are documented only through church records, family Bibles, cemetery inscriptions, and probate files.
- **Marriages:** County-level bonds and licenses survive from when each county was formed. FamilySearch's **Alabama, County Marriages, 1809–1950** (612,345 records; indexed + images) is the primary online source — earliest entries date to 1809 in Washington and Baldwin Counties.

### Church records
- **Catholic (Mobile area):** Parish registers of the Cathedral of the Immaculate Conception survive **from 1703** — baptisms, marriages, and burials in French (to 1763) and Spanish (to 1813). These are the most systematic vital-event records for the period and cover the entire Mobile colonial community.
  - *Held:* Archdiocese of Mobile Archives; microfilm at Mobile Municipal Archives; some on FamilySearch
- **Protestant:** Methodist and Baptist circuit riders arrived ~1808. Early records are irregular. FamilySearch's **Alabama, Church Records, 1803–1950** (156,789 records; indexed + images) covers what has been digitized.

### Census records
- **Federal census:** 1800 and 1810 enumerations covered the Mississippi Territory and include present-day Alabama (heads of household only). The **1820 census is the first taken under the State of Alabama**.
- **Territorial censuses (critical substitute):** Mississippi Territory conducted enumerations in **1801, 1805, 1808, and 1810** — often more detailed than federal censuses for this area.
  - *Held at:* ADAH, Montgomery; partially indexed in FamilySearch's **Alabama, Compiled Census and Census Substitutes Index, 1810–1890** (287,634 records)
- **Tax lists:** Annual territorial and county tax lists from c. 1800 are the **best substitute** for non-census years. They name heads of household, property values, and slaves.

### Probate and court records
- Records begin when each county was formed (Washington County, 1800).
- FamilySearch's **Alabama, Probate Records, 1809–1985** (438,210 records; indexed + images) — earliest entries from 1809.
- Probate files routinely name spouses, heirs, and creditors — invaluable when vital records are absent.

### Land records *(single most important record type for this period)*
- Alabama is a **federal-land (public-domain) state**. Land was originally owned by the U.S. government and conveyed by federal patent.
- **BLM GLO Records website** (glorecords.blm.gov) — free, searchable by name — holds indexed federal land patents from the Huntsville Land Office (opened 1809) and St. Stephens Land Office. Each patent anchors an ancestor to a specific township, range, and section.
- **Spanish and British colonial grants** (southern Alabama/Mobile): originals at the Archivo General de Indias, Seville; transcripts at the Library of Congress; some microfilm at Mobile Municipal Archives.

### Military records
- **Relevant conflicts:** Creek War (1813–1814); War of 1812 (1812–1815); territorial militia service.
- **Pension files** are especially rich: Creek War veterans who applied for pensions submitted detailed personal declarations naming family members, residence history, and personal narrative.
- *Held:* NARA; Fold3 (subscription) for digitized service records and pension files; ADAH for territorial militia rolls.

### Tax records
- Territorial tax lists from c. 1800 onward are the primary census substitute. They name heads of household and enumerate taxable property.
- *Held:* ADAH (primary); some FHL microfilm; otherwise physical only.

### Spanish and French colonial records *(special layer for Mobile-area families)*

| Record type | Repository | Access |
|-------------|-----------|--------|
| Spanish censuses (padrones) | Archivo General de Indias, Seville + Library of Congress | Physical; partial digital via PARES portal |
| Spanish church registers, Mobile | Archdiocese of Mobile Archives; Mobile Municipal Archives microfilm | Microfilm / in-person |
| French colonial registers, Mobile (1702–1763) | Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer, Aix-en-Provence | Physical; some microfilm |
| British West Florida records (1763–1780) | British National Archives, Kew + Library of Congress | Physical only |

---

## Repository guide

### Online repositories (free)

| Repository | What's here for this period |
|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | County Marriages 1809+; Probate 1809+; Church Records 1803+; Census Substitutes Index 1810+; 1800 & 1810 MS Territory federal censuses |
| **BLM GLO Records** (glorecords.blm.gov) | Federal land patents — all Alabama land offices, indexed by name |
| **Chronicling America** (loc.gov/chroniclingamerica) | Early territorial newspapers (sparse before 1815); Natchez and New Orleans papers that may name Alabama settlers |
| **Find A Grave** (findagrave.com) | Cemetery transcriptions statewide (pre-1820 entries sparse) |
| **PARES** (pares.culturaydeporte.gob.es) | Partial digital access to Archivo General de Indias (Spanish colonial records) |

### Online repositories (subscription)

| Repository | What's here |
|---|---|
| **Ancestry** | U.S. Federal Census 1800, 1810, 1820; various Alabama state collections |
| **Fold3** | War of 1812 service records and pension files; Creek War records |
| **Newspapers.com** | Historical newspapers including Alabama territorial papers |

### Physical repositories

| Repository | Key holdings |
|---|---|
| **Alabama Dept. of Archives & History (ADAH)**, Montgomery | Territorial censuses 1801–1810; tax lists; militia rolls; county records on microfilm |
| **NARA**, Washington D.C. / Atlanta | Federal land entry files; military service records; pension files (RG 15) |
| **County probate courts** | Original deed books, wills, estate files, marriage records from county formation |
| **Archdiocese of Mobile Archives** | Catholic parish registers from 1703 (baptism, marriage, burial) |
| **Mobile Municipal Archives** | Microfilm of French and Spanish colonial records |
| **Library of Congress** | Transcripts of Spanish colonial records; British West Florida records |
| **Archivo General de Indias** (Seville, Spain) | Original Spanish colonial records — censuses, land grants, church registers |
| **Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer** (Aix-en-Provence, France) | Original French colonial records for Mobile 1702–1763 |
| **Alabama Genealogical Society** | Published indexes and transcriptions of county records, many pre-statehood |

---

## Records NOT online — do not assume they don't exist

- Territorial tax lists (1800–1817) — ADAH and FHL microfilm; not name-searchable online
- Territorial court minutes and case files — ADAH and county courthouses; not digitized
- Spanish census returns (padrones), Mobile District — originals in Seville; Library of Congress transcripts
- French colonial registers, Mobile (1702–1763) — Aix-en-Provence; Mobile Municipal Archives microfilm
- British West Florida records (1763–1780) — British National Archives, Kew
- County deed books before c. 1809 — physical only at courthouses
- Territorial militia rolls — ADAH; not digitized
- Early Protestant church records before c. 1808 — scattered in local church archives

---

## Known record losses and gaps

| Gap | Substitute strategy |
|-----|---------------------|
| No statewide birth/death registration before 1908 | Church registers, probate records, cemetery inscriptions, family Bibles |
| No organized counties in most of present-day Alabama before 1800 | Territorial censuses; militia musters; land entry files at NARA/BLM |
| Some early Washington County courthouse records damaged or lost | Territorial records at ADAH; FHL microfilm |
| Sparse pre-1808 Protestant church records | Tax lists; federal census; probate records |
| No English-language press in present-day Alabama before ~1811 | Natchez and New Orleans papers on Chronicling America |

---

## Ten research tips for this jurisdiction

1. **Start with BLM GLO.** A federal land patent anchors your ancestor to a precise location and is usually the earliest U.S. document you'll find. Work outward from there.

2. **Identify the county of formation.** Washington (1800), Madison (1808), Baldwin (1809), Clarke (1812) — direct record requests to the courthouse or its ADAH microfilm equivalent for the county that existed at the event date.

3. **Territorial censuses fill the gap before 1820.** The Mississippi Territory enumerations of 1801, 1805, 1808, and 1810 at ADAH are often more informative than the federal census for this region.

4. **For Mobile-area families, Spanish and French records are indispensable.** Catholic registers survive from 1703 and include the whole colonial community. Don't skip the Archdiocese of Mobile Archives.

5. **Mine pension files.** Creek War and War of 1812 pension applications (NARA RG 15; Fold3) contain personal declarations with family data and residence histories that can span decades.

6. **Do FAN club research.** In a sparse territory, land files, tax lists, and court records name nearby settlers who are likely relatives, witnesses, or business associates. Researching neighbors is often the only way forward.

7. **Trace migration backwards.** Most Anglo-American settlers came from Georgia, South Carolina, and North Carolina. If Alabama records are thin, search the state of origin for birth, marriage, and early land records.

8. **Tax lists are your annual census substitute.** An ancestor who appears in the 1800 federal census and the 1809 land patent can be tracked year by year via territorial and county tax rolls in between.

9. **The FamilySearch Wiki article "Alabama Genealogy"** (familysearch.org/en/wiki/Alabama\_Genealogy) — particularly the "Territorial Records" and "Colonial Records" sections — is the recommended starting reference guide.

10. **Check the Alabama Genealogical Society** (alabamagenealogy.org) — their magazine contains county record transcriptions, many covering the pre-statehood period, unavailable anywhere online.

# Locality Guide: Arkansas Region — Spanish Colonial Period (1763–1800)

## Jurisdiction overview

The Arkansas region before 1800 was not an independent jurisdiction — it was a remote outpost of **Spanish Louisiana**, governed from New Orleans. A few facts set the stage for everything that follows about records:

- **Arkansas Post** (El Puesto de Arkansaw), on the lower Arkansas River, was the *only* permanent European settlement in the region. Established by the French in 1686, it passed to Spain after the **Treaty of Paris (1763)**.
- Spanish authority lasted until **1800** (Treaty of San Ildefonso), when Louisiana was secretly retroceded to France, then sold to the U.S. in 1803.
- The civilian population was **tiny** — perhaps 100–400 European settlers at any given time: French Creole farming families, Spanish military officers and soldiers, fur traders, Catholic missionaries, and enslaved Africans. The surrounding territory was predominantly Quapaw and other Native peoples.
- All governance flowed through the **post commandant**, who exercised civil, military, and judicial authority locally. There were no counties; no county-level records exist before 1813.

---

## Boundary changes (what directly affects records)

| Period | Governing authority | Where records were created |
|---|---|---|
| 1686–1763 | French Louisiana | Arkansas Post; Paris/Aix-en-Provence archives |
| 1763–1800 | Spanish Louisiana | Arkansas Post; New Orleans; Seville (Spain) |
| 1800–1803 | French Louisiana (brief) | Transition period; minimal new records |
| 1803–1812 | U.S. Louisiana/Orleans Territory | Territorial records begin |
| 1812–1819 | Missouri Territory | |
| 1819– | Arkansas Territory → State (1836) | County records begin |

**Bottom line:** For any ancestor in Arkansas before 1800, you are searching a Spanish colonial administrative system, not American records. The records follow the Spanish bureaucratic chain — from the local commandant up to the Governor-General in New Orleans, and ultimately to the *Papeles de Cuba* collection in Seville.

---

## Available record types

### 1. Church Records (Sacramental Registers) — *The primary vital record source*

Civil registration of births, marriages, and deaths did **not exist**. The Catholic Church's registers are the only systematic vital record for the entire colonial period (and for decades after).

- **Denomination:** Roman Catholic — Capuchin Franciscan missionaries
- **Record types:** Baptisms (settlers, some Native Americans, enslaved persons), marriages (with witnesses named), burials
- **Language:** Latin and Spanish; some French-language entries in earlier records
- **Date range:** French-period registers begin c. early 1700s; Spanish-period registers 1763–1800

**Access:**

| Copy | Location | Access level |
|---|---|---|
| Digitized volume (64 images, 1763–1800) | FamilySearch image group `008814402` | **Browse-only** — 0% indexed, not full-text searchable, in Spanish/Latin |
| Original/early-copy registers | Roman Catholic Diocese of Little Rock | Physical — contact archivist |
| Transcribed copies | Arkansas History Commission, Little Rock | Physical / archivist inquiry |

> **How to reach the FamilySearch volume:** Go to FamilySearch.org → Catalog → search "Arkansas Post" → locate the sacramental register film. There is no name index; every image must be browsed manually. Spanish/Latin reading ability or a translation aid is essential. At 64 images this is manageable to browse completely.

---

### 2. Padrones (Spanish Colonial Censuses)

*Padrones* were household censuses ordered by the Governor-General and conducted by the post commandant. They list heads of household, family members by name, age, sex, and racial/ethnic status, and sometimes occupation or land held.

- **Who appears:** All heads of household and their family members — civilian settlers, soldiers, enslaved persons
- **Frequency:** Irregular; known padrones survive from the 1760s–1790s
- **Language:** Spanish

**Access:**

| Copy | Location | Access level |
|---|---|---|
| Originals | Archivo General de Indias, Seville (*Papeles de Cuba* section) | Physical; partially on PARES portal |
| Microfilm | Library of Congress, Manuscript Division | Physical / microfilm order |
| Transcriptions | Arkansas History Commission | Physical / archivist inquiry |
| Published transcriptions | Winston De Ville and other Louisiana colonial researchers (in print) | WorldCat / genealogical libraries |

---

### 3. Land Records (Spanish Concessions)

Spanish land grants (*concesiones*) were issued by the Governor-General for larger tracts or by the local commandant for smaller plots.

**Two-stage access strategy:**

**Stage 1 — Colonial originals (in Spain):**
- Archivo General de Indias, *Papeles de Cuba* — contains petitions, grants, and surveys
- Library of Congress microfilm — extensive coverage of Louisiana/Arkansas grants

**Stage 2 — U.S. confirmation records (the more accessible shortcut):**
After 1803, the U.S. Board of Land Commissioners reviewed every Spanish-era claim. These records describe the original grant and name the original grantee — making them an effective substitute when the Seville originals are out of reach.

| Source | Location | Access level |
|---|---|---|
| *American State Papers: Public Lands*, Vols. 2–3 | memory.loc.gov (Library of Congress); HathiTrust | **Free online** — fully digitized; search by name |
| BLM General Land Office patent records | glorecords.blm.gov | **Free online** — searchable database |
| Board of Land Commissioners records | NARA (Washington D.C. / Fort Worth) | Physical / NARA catalog |

> **Tip:** The *American State Papers* is one of the best and most overlooked genealogical resources for this region. Each confirmed or rejected claim names the original Spanish grantee, date of concession, location, and often witnesses or heirs. Start here before attempting Seville.

---

### 4. Military Records

Arkansas Post maintained a Spanish garrison. Officers were often Spanish-born; soldiers were frequently French Creole or of mixed heritage.

- **Record types:** Muster rolls (*listas de revista*), service records, correspondence, officer biographical data
- **Where held:** Archivo General de Indias (*Papeles de Cuba*); Archivo General de Simancas (Valladolid, Spain)
- **Library of Congress** — microfilm of Papeles de Cuba portions
- **Access level:** Physical or microfilm only; no indexed digital collections found

---

### 5. Civil and Judicial Records

Contracts, wills, inventories, and local judicial proceedings were handled by the post commandant and forwarded up the chain to New Orleans and Havana/Seville.

- **Where held:** Archivo General de Indias (*Papeles de Cuba*)
- **Access level:** Physical or microfilm at Library of Congress

---

### 6. U.S.-era records (for descendant research)

These post-colonial records are useful for tracing families forward from the colonial period:

| Collection | FamilySearch ID | Coverage | Access |
|---|---|---|---|
| Arkansas Compiled Census & Census Substitutes | 2085124 | 1819–1870, 178,345 records | Indexed + images |
| Arkansas County Marriages | 1920452 | 1837–1957, 389,210 records | Indexed + images |

The first U.S. federal census enumerating Arkansas is **1830**. A territorial census was taken in **1823**.

---

## Repository guide

### Online repositories

| Repository | What's there for this period | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | 1 browse-only volume: Arkansas Post sacramental registers 1763–1800, 64 images (image group `008814402`) | Free |
| **PARES** (pares.culturaydeporte.gob.es) | Partially digitized *Papeles de Cuba*; search "Arkansaw" or "Arkansas" | Free |
| **BLM GLO** (glorecords.blm.gov) | Confirmed Spanish land grant patents in Arkansas | Free |
| **LOC** (memory.loc.gov) | *American State Papers: Public Lands* — confirmed Spanish grant lists | Free |

### Physical repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Archivo General de Indias** — Seville, Spain | *Papeles de Cuba*: padrones, land records, military rolls, civil/judicial records for Spanish Louisiana including Arkansas Post, 1763–1800. **The single most important repository for this period.** | In-person or hire local researcher; PARES portal for partially digitized docs |
| **Library of Congress**, Manuscript Division — Washington, D.C. | Microfilm of *Papeles de Cuba* (Louisiana/Arkansas coverage) | In-person; some microfilm reproduction orders possible |
| **Arkansas History Commission** (State Archives) — Little Rock, AR | Transcribed colonial records, church register abstracts, early territorial records | In-person or archivist inquiry; arkansasstatearchives.com |
| **Roman Catholic Diocese of Little Rock** — Little Rock, AR | Original/early-copy sacramental registers from Arkansas Post | By appointment with archivist |
| **NARA** — Washington, D.C. / Fort Worth, TX | U.S. Board of Land Commissioners records; territorial court records post-1803 | In-person or NARA catalog (catalog.archives.gov) |
| **Arkansas Post National Memorial** — Gillett, AR | Research library; staff referrals to specialists | In-person or contact site |

---

## Records NOT online

The following records **exist** for this period but are **not** in any freely searchable online database:

- **Padrones** (Spanish colonial censuses) — Library of Congress microfilm and Seville originals only
- **Spanish land concession originals** — Seville; U.S. confirmations partially online via American State Papers and BLM GLO
- **Spanish military records** (muster rolls, service records) — Seville and Simancas
- **Colonial civil and judicial records** (contracts, wills, inventories) — Seville
- **Full sacramental registers** beyond the 64-image FamilySearch volume — Diocese of Little Rock and Arkansas History Commission

---

## Known record losses and gaps

- **Sparse population = sparse records.** Entire decades may yield only a handful of entries in surviving registers. Non-appearance does not mean the ancestor was absent — they may simply not have had a recorded event.
- **Incomplete microfilming of the *Papeles de Cuba*.** The Library of Congress filmed large portions but not everything. Some documents remain un-filmed in Seville.
- **No county records before 1813.** Arkansas County was not formed until 1813; there is no county-level record series before that date.
- **French-period records (1686–1763)** predate Spanish administration. These may be at the **Archives nationales d'outre-mer (ANOM)** in Aix-en-Provence, France, and in the Louisiana Colonial Records collections in New Orleans.
- **Transition losses.** The repeated sovereignty transfers (France→Spain→France→U.S.) created conditions for record loss; not all commandants forwarded records correctly.

---

## Research tips

1. **Start with the FamilySearch browse-only volume.** Image group `008814402` (64 images, sacramental registers 1763–1800) is the most immediately accessible source. Browse every image — there is no index. Use a translation aid for the Spanish/Latin text.

2. **The *Papeles de Cuba* is the key archive.** Before planning a trip to Seville, check the **Library of Congress microfilm** first — much of the relevant content is on film in Washington. Try the **PARES portal** (pares.culturaydeporte.gob.es) for partially digitized documents; search "Arkansaw" (the Spanish spelling) as well as "Arkansas."

3. **Use the *American State Papers* for land clues.** Each confirmed Spanish land claim names the original grantee, concession date, location, and often witnesses or heirs. Freely available at memory.loc.gov. This is one of the best and most overlooked sources for this research problem.

4. **Consult published transcription projects.** Louisiana colonial genealogist **Winston De Ville** published transcriptions of Arkansas Post padrones and other records. Similar projects exist through the **Louisiana Genealogical and Historical Society**. Check WorldCat and the Arkansas History Commission's bibliography.

5. **Think regionally — New Orleans records matter.** Arkansas Post was subordinate to New Orleans. Land grants above a certain size, appeals, and provincial-level decisions are in New Orleans materials. The **Louisiana Historical Center** (Louisiana State Museum) and the **New Orleans Notarial Archives** hold Spanish colonial Louisiana materials that may name Arkansas Post residents.

6. **All settlers were Catholic.** If an ancestor appears in any record at Arkansas Post, they were almost certainly Catholic or recorded by Catholic missionaries. Church registers are not just a substitute for civil registration — they are the *only* systematic vital record that existed.

7. **Record language:** Spanish-period records are in Spanish or Latin. French Creole settlers appear under French-language surname forms even in Spanish records (e.g., *Fontenot*, *Dardenne*, *Dumont*). Be prepared for highly variable spelling.

8. **DNA as a supplement.** Given the documentary scarcity, autosomal and Y-DNA testing can corroborate or suggest family connections that documents cannot prove. Compare tested descendants against known Creole/colonial Louisiana and Arkansas lines.

9. **Contact Arkansas Post National Memorial** (Gillett, AR). Staff have deep familiarity with the site's history and can refer you to specialized researchers and resources not easily found elsewhere.

---

*Guide written 2026-06-19. Sources: FamilySearch Wiki "Arkansas Genealogy" (Colonial Records, Church Records sections); FamilySearch volume search (image group 008814402); FamilySearch collections search; Wikipedia "Arkansas"; Arkansas History Commission; Archivo General de Indias finding aids; American State Papers: Public Lands.*

---

A few things to flag that shape your strategy for this period:

**The population was so small that the records that *do* survive are worth examining exhaustively.** The FamilySearch sacramental register volume (image group `008814402`) is only 64 images — that's entirely browsable in an afternoon. Start there.

**Almost nothing is indexed for this period.** The 3 FamilySearch collections for Arkansas all start in 1819 or later. For pre-1800 research you are in browse-only or physical-access territory almost exclusively.

**The *Papeles de Cuba* in Seville is the motherlode** — but before booking a flight, exhaust the Library of Congress microfilm and the PARES portal, and check the *American State Papers* for land records. These are all accessible from home.

---

# Locality Guide: Ohio / Northwest Territory (c. 1785–1803)

> **Research period note:** Ohio did not exist as a jurisdiction in 1790. The controlling authority was the **Northwest Territory**, established by the Northwest Ordinance of 1787. All records created before 1803 must be sought through the Territory's two organized counties and federal land-office systems — not through any Ohio state repository.

---

## Jurisdiction Overview

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Governing authority (1790)** | Northwest Territory (U.S. federal territory, est. 1787) |
| **Territorial capital** | Marietta, Ohio (1788–1800); Cincinnati (1800–1803) |
| **Statehood** | March 1, 1803 — Ohio became the 17th state |
| **Parent ordinance** | Northwest Ordinance of 1787 |
| **Population c. 1790** | ~3,000–5,000 non-Indigenous settlers, concentrated near Marietta and early Cincinnati/Losantiville |
| **Economy** | Subsistence farming, fur trade, land speculation, military provisioning |
| **Ethnic/national groups** | New Englanders (Ohio Company settlers), Virginians/Kentuckians (Symmes Purchase), Pennsylvania Germans and Scots-Irish overland migrants |
| **Religious denominations** | Congregationalist, Presbyterian, Methodist circuit, Baptist, Quaker — very few organized congregations before 1795 |
| **Key events** | Marietta founded 1788; Battle of Fallen Timbers 1794; Treaty of Greenville 1795 (opened Ohio to safe settlement); Indiana Territory split off 1800 |

---

## Boundary Changes

Understanding the jurisdictional sequence is the **single most important step** in pre-statehood Ohio research. Records follow the jurisdiction that created them, not modern county lines.

### Territorial county sequence around 1790

| Date | Event | Coverage |
|---|---|---|
| July 13, 1787 | Northwest Ordinance enacted | All of future Ohio was unorganized federal territory |
| July 27, 1788 | **Washington County** organized | Covered virtually ALL of present-day Ohio — seat at Marietta |
| Jan. 2, 1790 | **Hamilton County** carved from Washington County | Southwestern Ohio (Cincinnati area) |
| Aug. 15, 1795 | **Wayne County** formed | Most of present northwestern Ohio and what became Indiana Territory |
| July 10, 1796 | **Trumbull County** formed | Northeastern Ohio (Connecticut Western Reserve) |
| May 7, 1800 | Indiana Territory split off | Ohio's pre-statehood territory was consolidated |
| March 1, 1803 | **Ohio statehood** | Territorial government dissolved |

**Practical rule for 1790:** If your ancestor was in southeastern/central Ohio → look in **Washington County** records. If southwestern Ohio → **Hamilton County** (from Jan. 1790), Washington County before that. For much of the territory, no county organization existed at all.

### The five land districts — a parallel boundary system

Ohio's land was allocated under **five distinct survey systems**, each with its own records and repository:

| District | Location | Survey System | Key Repository |
|---|---|---|---|
| Seven Ranges | Easternmost strip (Jefferson, Columbiana cos.) | Federal rectangular survey (first in U.S., 1785–87) | BLM GLO online |
| Ohio Company Purchase | Southeast (Washington, Athens, Meigs cos.) | Federal township-range | Ohio History Connection; BLM GLO |
| Symmes/Miami Purchase | Southwest (Hamilton, Butler, Warren cos.) | Irregular metes-and-bounds | BLM GLO; Hamilton Co. recorder |
| **Virginia Military District** | Between Scioto and Little Miami rivers (Ross, Pickaway, Fayette cos.) | **Metes and bounds — no rectangular townships** | **Virginia State Archives** (warrants); BLM GLO (surveys) |
| **Connecticut Western Reserve** | Northeastern Ohio (Trumbull, Geauga, Cuyahoga cos.) | Connecticut township-range grid | Western Reserve Historical Society, Cleveland |
| U.S. Military District | Central Ohio (Licking, Muskingum, Knox cos.) | Rectangular; bounty land for Continental Army | BLM GLO; NARA |

> **Critical:** If your ancestor settled in the Virginia Military District, the original bounty-land warrant was issued by Virginia — search the Library of Virginia (lva.virginia.gov, many records free online) *before* searching any Ohio repository.

---

## Available Record Types

### Vital Records (Civil Registration)
- **Start date:** Ohio had **no civil registration** before or at 1790. County-level marriage recording began with Washington County (1789) and Hamilton County (1790). Statewide birth/death registration began 1867, not reliable until ~1908.
- **For 1785–1803:** County marriage bonds and registers only — no civil birth or death records whatsoever exist.
- **Pre-registration alternatives:** Church registers are the only vital-event source.

**Digitization level — marriages: Indexed + images**
*FamilySearch "Ohio, County Marriages, 1789–2013" — 1,523,678 records, 761,839 images — includes the earliest Washington and Hamilton County registers.*

---

### Church Records
- **Denominations c. 1790:** Congregationalist (New England Ohio Company settlers), Presbyterian, Methodist circuit riders (rarely kept formal registers before 1800), Baptist, Quaker
- **Caution:** Very few congregations were formally organized before 1795. Most 1788–1795 settlers were served by itinerant preachers with no parish register kept. The Treaty of Greenville triggered the main settlement wave; organized churches followed a few years later.
- **Where held:** Local congregations; Ohio History Connection; FamilySearch microfilm; United Methodist Archives (Drew University); Presbyterian Historical Society (Philadelphia); Ohio Yearly Meeting (Quaker) records at Mount Pleasant, Ohio

**Digitization level: Indexed + images (partial)**
*FamilySearch "Ohio, Church Records, 1787–1950" — 678,234 records. Coverage is uneven; many small early congregations are absent.*

---

### Census Records
- **1790 federal census: Does NOT cover Ohio.** The Northwest Territory was not enumerated in 1790. **There is no 1790 census for your Ohio ancestors.**
- **1800 federal census:** The first federal census covering Ohio Territory — exists and is searchable. This is the earliest census document for virtually all Ohio settlers.
- **State censuses:** Ohio conducted none.
- **Substitute for 1790–1799:** Tax lists (see below).

**Digitization level — 1800 census: Indexed + images** (Ancestry, FamilySearch, Fold3)

---

### Tax Records
Tax lists are the **most important substitute source** for the missing 1790 census. Washington County taxed residents from c. 1788; Hamilton County from 1790.

- **Types:** Real property (land), personal property (livestock, goods), poll (adult males)
- **Where held:** Ohio History Connection (pre-statehood and early state lists); county auditor; FamilySearch (partial)

**Digitization levels:**
- *FamilySearch "Ohio, Tax Records, 1800–1850" — 345,890 records:* **Indexed + images** (begins at 1800, not before)
- *Washington County Tax Records 1799–1803 (FamilySearch image group 005112240):* 73 digitized images, **12% indexed — mostly browse-only.** Access via FamilySearch Catalog.
- Pre-1799 territorial tax lists: **Physical only** at Ohio History Connection, or microfilm via FamilySearch Catalog

---

### Land Records
Land records are the **richest source category** for pre-statehood Ohio and often the only documentary trace of a settler before 1800.

- **System:** Federal public-land state — original entries were federal patents. Exception: Virginia Military District (Virginia-issued warrants, metes-and-bounds).
- **Types:** Federal land patents/entries; Virginia bounty land warrants and surveys; Connecticut Western Reserve grants; Symmes Purchase entries; county deed books (after initial patent)
- **Earliest entries:** 1785 (Seven Ranges survey); Ohio Company Purchase from 1788; Symmes Purchase from 1789

**Digitization levels:**
- Federal patents: **Indexed + images — free** at BLM GLO (glorecords.blm.gov)
- Virginia Military District warrants: **Indexed + images** (Library of Virginia portal; Ancestry)
- *FamilySearch "Ohio, Land Records, 1787–1840" — 234,567 records:* **Indexed + images**
- *Northwest Territory Land Records 1788–1799 (FamilySearch image group 005112233):* 51 digitized images, **0% indexed, not full-text searchable — browse-only.** Access image by image via FamilySearch Catalog.
- County deed books not in the above: **Microfilm** (FamilySearch Catalog) or **physical only**

---

### Probate and Court Records
- **Types:** Wills, estate inventories, letters of administration, guardianships, civil suits, territorial court orders
- **Date range:** Washington County Common Pleas from 1788; Hamilton County from 1790
- **Volume:** Very thin for 1788–1800. Active frontier conflict and small population mean few probate cases before 1800. The Marietta court generated the earliest surviving territorial records.
- **Where held:** Ohio History Connection (territorial court records and early county minutes); Washington County Courthouse (Marietta); Hamilton County Courthouse (Cincinnati); FamilySearch microfilm for some early volumes

**Digitization level: Largely microfilm or physical only.** No major indexed online collection for pre-1803 Ohio probate. Check FamilySearch Catalog → "Washington County, Ohio — Probate records."

---

### Military Records
- **Relevant conflicts:** Revolutionary War (many settlers were veterans — the reason the Virginia Military District and U.S. Military District exist); Northwest Indian Wars/Fallen Timbers campaign 1790–1794; territorial militia musters
- **Record types:** Revolutionary War pension files (NARA M804); Revolutionary War bounty land warrants; Northwest Indian Wars service records; territorial militia musters

**Digitization levels:**
- Revolutionary War pension files: **Indexed + images** (Fold3, Ancestry, FamilySearch)
- Bounty land warrants (federal): **Indexed + images** (BLM GLO; Fold3)
- Virginia-issued warrants: **Indexed + images** (Library of Virginia)
- Territorial militia musters: **Microfilm or physical only** (Ohio History Connection)

> **Top research tip:** Always search Revolutionary War pension files for any early Ohio settler. The pension narrative — written by the veteran or his widow in the 1820s–1840s — frequently contains the most detailed biography ever found: birthplace, prior states of residence, wife's name, children, and dates.

---

### Cemetery Records
- Formal cemeteries were rare before 1800. Most settlers used family plots on their land grants. The first organized cemeteries appeared in Marietta (Mound Cemetery, est. 1801) and Cincinnati.
- FindAGrave has broad Ohio coverage but very limited pre-1810 entries — most headstones from this period did not survive.
- The DAR and Ohio Genealogical Society have published transcription volumes for many pioneer cemeteries.

**Digitization level: Partial indexed** (FindAGrave); many pioneer graves are **physical only** or transcription-only

---

### Newspaper Records
- **First Ohio newspaper:** *Centinel of the North-Western Territory* (Cincinnati), begun November 9, 1793 — the first newspaper west of the Allegheny Mountains
- **Marietta:** *The Ohio Gazette* began 1801
- **Content:** Legal notices, militia calls, land announcements — rare personal genealogical data at this early date
- **Where held:** Ohio History Connection; Chronicling America (chroniclingamerica.loc.gov); Newspapers.com (later runs)

**Digitization level: Browse-only images** for earliest issues; later increasingly full-text searchable via Chronicling America (free)

---

## Repository Guide

### Online Repositories

| Repository | Collections for this place/period | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | County Marriages 1789–2013; Church Records 1787–1950; Land Records 1787–1840; Tax Records 1800–1850; browse-only territorial volumes | Free |
| **BLM GLO** (glorecords.blm.gov) | All federal Ohio land patents by name — Ohio Company, Seven Ranges, Symmes, U.S. Military District | Free |
| **Library of Virginia** (lva.virginia.gov) | Virginia Military District warrants and surveys | Free |
| **Fold3** | Revolutionary War pension files (M804); military service records | Subscription |
| **Ancestry** | 1800 federal census; Rev. War pensions; Virginia Military District warrants; Ohio county records | Subscription |
| **Chronicling America** (loc.gov) | *Centinel of the North-Western Territory* 1793+; early Ohio papers | Free |
| **NARA** (archives.gov) | Federal territorial papers; pension files; federal land records | In-person + some online via partners |

### Physical Repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Ohio History Connection**, Columbus, OH | Territorial court records; pre-statehood county records; militia musters; DAR/OGS transcriptions | In-person; mail/email requests available |
| **Washington County Courthouse**, Marietta, OH | Deed books, marriage records, court minutes from 1788 | In-person |
| **Hamilton County Courthouse**, Cincinnati, OH | County records from 1790 | In-person |
| **Western Reserve Historical Society**, Cleveland, OH | Connecticut Western Reserve grants, Trumbull County early records | In-person; some microfilm via FamilySearch |
| **Library of Virginia**, Richmond, VA | Virginia Military District warrants, surveys, plats | In-person; many online |
| **NARA**, Washington, D.C. / College Park | Federal territorial papers; land records; military records | In-person; some online via Fold3/Ancestry |
| **United Methodist Archives**, Drew University, NJ | Methodist circuit records for early Ohio | In-person / correspondence |
| **Presbyterian Historical Society**, Philadelphia, PA | Presbyterian session records, early Ohio congregations | In-person / online catalog |

---

## Records NOT Online

These record types are known to exist but have **no significant indexed online presence** — a researcher who searches only online databases will miss them entirely:

- **Pre-1799 territorial tax lists** — Ohio History Connection; microfilm via FamilySearch Catalog
- **Washington County pre-1800 probate and court minutes** — courthouse; microfilm
- **Hamilton County pre-1800 probate and court minutes** — courthouse; microfilm
- **Territorial militia muster rolls** — Ohio History Connection
- **Northwest Territory Land Records 1788–1799** (FS image group 005112233) — 51 images, 0% indexed, browse-only
- **Washington County Tax Records 1799–1803** (FS image group 005112240) — 88% of images unindexed, browse-only for most entries
- **Virginia Military District survey plats** (originals) — Library of Virginia
- **Ohio Company Purchase land-entry papers** — Ohio History Connection
- **Early church registers** for congregations not yet microfilmed

---

## Known Record Losses

| Loss | Details | Substitute |
|---|---|---|
| **No 1790 federal census** | Northwest Territory was not enumerated | 1788–1790 tax lists; land-entry records; militia musters |
| **No civil birth/death records before 1867** | Civil registration did not exist in Ohio at this period | Church baptism/burial registers; probate estate inventories; gravestones |
| **Frontier disruption 1790–1795** | Northwest Indian Wars displaced settlers and disrupted record-keeping | Post-1795 records that retrospectively mention earlier events; pension narratives |
| **Early court volumes lost or damaged** | Washington County had 19th-century courthouse fires and floods | Ohio History Connection holdings; microfilm taken before losses |
| **Symmes Purchase title irregularities** | Symmes sold land he lacked clear title to; some grantees' records are disputed | BLM GLO federal survey records supersede Symmes records for legal title |

---

## Research Tips

1. **Establish the land district first.** Search BLM GLO by ancestor name — the legal description on the patent immediately tells you which district applies and which repository holds the original warrant.

2. **Search Revolutionary War pension files before anything else** for suspected veterans. The pension narrative is often the richest biographical document available for an early Ohio settler — birthplace, prior states, wife's name, children, and dates — far surpassing what any census or vital record offers.

3. **Use tax lists as a census substitute for 1788–1800.** Washington County tax lists from c. 1788 and Hamilton County lists from 1790 document adult male heads of household by name, land acreage, and personal property — the closest functional equivalent to a census for this period.

4. **For the Virginia Military District, search Virginia first.** Many settlers did not receive their federal patent until years after settling. The Library of Virginia (lva.virginia.gov) has warrants and surveys online, often free.

5. **Browse the unindexed FamilySearch volumes.** Image groups 005112233 (Northwest Territory Land Records 1788–1799, 0% indexed) and 005112240 (Washington County Tax Records 1799–1803, 12% indexed) contain records invisible to name searches. Access via FamilySearch Catalog → place search.

6. **Settlement after 1795 is far better documented than before it.** The Treaty of Greenville was a turning point — land patents, tax lists, county courts, and church registers all expand rapidly after that date. Fragmentary records before 1795 are the norm, not an exception.

7. **Check the Ohio Genealogical Society** (ogs.org) and its county chapters for published transcription volumes covering early tax lists, cemetery surveys, and pioneer family histories — many record sets that have never been digitized exist only in these publications.

8. **FamilySearch Wiki article to read:** "Ohio Genealogy" — especially sections "Northwest Territory and Boundary Changes" and "Land Records."
[familysearch.org/en/wiki/Ohio_Genealogy](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Ohio_Genealogy)

---

*Guide written 2026-06-19. Sources: FamilySearch Wiki "Ohio Genealogy"; FamilySearch collections and volume search results; Wikipedia "Ohio" and "Northwest Territory"; BLM GLO and Ohio History Connection repository information. Saved to `ohio-northwest-territory-1790-locality-guide.md`.*

---

### How this guide answers your core questions

**On boundary changes:** In 1790, Ohio as a legal entity did not exist. The entire region was part of the **Northwest Territory** governed by Congress. Only two counties had been organized: Washington County (1788, covering most of Ohio, seat at Marietta) and Hamilton County (just created January 1790, covering the Cincinnati area). Any record your ancestor generated in 1790 is a *Washington County* or *Hamilton County* record — and those county-level records are themselves held partly at the Ohio History Connection in Columbus and partly at the original county courthouses. Adding another layer, the land-grant districts (Virginia Military District, Connecticut Western Reserve, Ohio Company Purchase, etc.) each have their own record sets at entirely different repositories, sometimes in different states.

**On gaps in early documentation:** The gaps are real and structural, not just digitization lag:
- There is **no 1790 census** for Ohio — the territory was not enumerated
- There are **no civil birth or death records** for this period at all
- Most settlers before 1795 were living on an active military frontier; few had time or opportunity to generate paper records beyond a land entry
- The best substitutes are **land-entry records** (BLM GLO, free online), **Revolutionary War pension files** (rich biographical narratives on Fold3/Ancestry), and **tax lists** (Ohio History Connection, partially microfilmed)

---

# Locality Guide: California — Gold Rush Era (1848–1860)

## Jurisdiction overview

| | |
|---|---|
| **Formed** | State of California admitted September 9, 1850 (skipped territorial status). Previously Mexican territory ceded by Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo, February 2, 1848. |
| **Population** | ~93,000 (1850 federal census, partial) → ~380,000 (1860 federal census). The Wikipedia summary of the Gold Rush notes ~300,000 migrants arrived between 1848 and 1855. |
| **Economy** | Placer and hard-rock gold mining in the Sierra Nevada foothills (Mother Lode); commerce and shipping in San Francisco and Sacramento. |
| **Ethnic composition** | Eastern U.S. Americans, Californios, Chinese (large numbers by 1852), Latin Americans (Chilean, Mexican, Peruvian), Irish, German, French, Cornish, Australian, African American. |
| **Religions** | Roman Catholic (dominant pre-Rush and continuing); Methodist, Presbyterian, Baptist, Congregationalist, Episcopal; Jewish (Congregation Emanu-El, San Francisco, est. 1850). |

---

## Boundary changes

Mining-county boundaries shifted rapidly throughout the 1850s. California had 27 original counties at statehood; El Dorado, Placer, Nevada, Tuolumne, Calaveras, Amador, and Mariposa counties were all carved out from parent counties by 1854. **Always confirm which county held jurisdiction over a specific mining locality at the event date** — a township in El Dorado County in 1850 may have transferred to Amador County by 1855. Historical county maps are at the David Rumsey Map Collection (rumsey.com) and the California State Library.

---

## Available record types

### 1. Vital records (civil registration)
- **Key fact:** No statewide mandatory registration until **1905**. County registration of births and marriages began at statehood (1850) but was voluntary and inconsistently practiced. Death registration began at county level in 1872.
- **FamilySearch — County Births, 1850–1919:** 789,456 indexed records with images → **Indexed + images**
- **FamilySearch — County Marriages, 1850–1952:** 567,890 indexed records (1.1M persons) with images → **Indexed + images**
- **Deaths before 1872:** No major indexed collection. Use mortality census schedules, newspapers, and church burial registers as substitutes.

### 2. The 1852 California State Census ⭐
This is **the single most important record for the Gold Rush period.** California conducted a unique state census in 1852 — covering the peak of the Rush — that no other state has for this decade.
- FamilySearch volume (imageGroupNumber 006601001): 522 images, **88% name-indexed, full-text searchable** → effectively **Indexed + images**
- Fills the gap left by the partially destroyed 1850 federal census.

### 3. Federal census records
- **1850 federal census:** Partially destroyed — returns for portions of Contra Costa, San Francisco, and Santa Clara counties are missing. Available indexed on Ancestry and FamilySearch.
- **1860 federal census:** Complete. Indexed + images on Ancestry and FamilySearch.
- **Mortality schedules (1850, 1860):** List individuals who died in the year preceding the census — a critical death-record substitute. Indexed + images on Ancestry and FamilySearch.

### 4. The Great Registers (voter registration) ⭐
- California's **most genealogically rich record type** for the Gold Rush generation.
- Required every male voter to register with: full name, age, **birthplace**, occupation, physical description (height, hair, eye color), and residence.
- FamilySearch — "California, Great Registers, 1866–1910": **1,345,678 indexed records** with images → **Indexed + images**
- The 1866 register was compiled just 16 years after the Rush; miners who stayed will appear with their stated origin state or country, filling the gap where no 1850s vital record exists.

### 5. Ship passenger manifests (immigration)
- San Francisco was the dominant port of entry. Hundreds of ships arrived monthly by 1850–1851.
- **NARA Pacific Region, San Bruno, CA** holds original manifests (1820–1957 San Francisco arrivals).
- Indexed on Ancestry ("California, San Francisco Area Passenger Lists, 1850–1882") and FamilySearch. Manifests record name, age, sex, occupation, and country of origin.
- **Sacramento Port Arrival Registers, 1849–1858:** FamilySearch volume (imageGroupNumber 006601003, 298 images) — **browse-only**, not indexed.
- Overland arrivals (California/Oregon Trail) left no systematic records. Consult emigrant company rosters and trail diaries at the Bancroft Library.

### 6. Mining claim records
- Filed with the county recorder in each mining county: claimant name, claim location, boundaries, date, witnesses.
- **Underutilized and often the only record placing a miner in a specific county and year.**
- **FamilySearch** — San Francisco area Mining Claim Registers, 1849–1855 (imageGroupNumber 006601002, 340 images): **browse-only**, 0% indexed → **Browse-only images**
- All other mining counties (El Dorado, Placer, Nevada, Tuolumne, etc.): **physical only** at county courthouses.

### 7. Mission records
- FamilySearch — "California, Mission Records, 1769–1850": **98,765 indexed records** with images. Baptisms, marriages, and burials from the 21 Franciscan missions → **Indexed + images**
- Relevant for Californio ancestry and Native California families.

### 8. Probate and court records
- Wills, estate files, guardianships, and civil/criminal cases — filed at county level from statehood (1850).
- **Naturalization records:** Before 1906, any court of record could process naturalizations. Check both county courthouses and NARA Pacific Region (San Bruno) for federal court naturalizations. Also partially on Ancestry and FamilySearch.
- Probate files often name heirs and birthplaces — critical when vital records are absent.
- Digitization level: **Largely physical only or microfilm**; some county records on Ancestry or FamilySearch.

### 9. Land records
- **BLM General Land Office** (glorecords.blm.gov): Federal land patents including 1850s cash-sale entries — **free, indexed online**
- **Mexican land grants (ranchos):** ~800 confirmed by U.S. Land Claims Commission, 1851–1856. Records at California State Archives and BLM. Essential for Californio ancestry.
- **County deed records and tax rolls:** Physical only at county courthouses and California State Archives.

### 10. Newspapers ⭐
- *Alta California* (San Francisco, 1849–1891), *Sacramento Union* (est. 1851), many mining camp papers.
- **California Digital Newspaper Collection** (cdnc.ucr.edu): **Free, full-text searchable** — the first place to look for death notices and marriage announcements, since death registration was nearly nonexistent before 1872.
- **Newspapers.com** (subscription): Broader coverage.
- California State Library holds extensive microfilm for papers not digitized.

### 11. Military records
- Mexican-American War veterans, California militia (1850s Indian Wars expeditions), Civil War California Volunteers.
- Federal service/pension records: **Indexed + images** on Fold3, Ancestry, and some on FamilySearch.
- California State Archives militia records: physical only.

### 12. City directories
- San Francisco Directory from 1850; Sacramento Directory from 1851. Annual snapshots of residents, addresses, and occupations — invaluable for tracking mobile miners who are not in census or vital records in a given year.
- Some digitized on Archive.org (free) or Ancestry (subscription); physical at California State Library and Bancroft Library.

### 13. Tax records (including Foreign Miners' Tax)
- Annual county assessment rolls from 1850: physical only at county assessor offices and California State Archives.
- **Foreign Miners' Tax registers (1850, 1852 laws):** The primary documentary source for Chinese and Latin American miners. Enumerate miners by name and location. Physical at county courthouses and California State Archives.

### 14. Fraternal organization records
- Masonic lodges and Odd Fellows chapters were among the first organizations established in mining towns. Lodge membership registers and death records at Grand Lodge of California archives. Physical only; written inquiry required.

---

## Repository guide

### Online

| Repository | Key holdings for this period | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | County Births 1850–1919 (789K); County Marriages 1850–1952 (568K); Great Registers 1866–1910 (1.35M); Mission Records 1769–1850 (99K); 1852 State Census (indexed); Mining Claim Registers + Port Arrivals (browse-only) | Free |
| **Ancestry** | Federal census 1850 & 1860; SF passenger lists; naturalization; city directories | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Military service and pension records; Civil War California volunteers | Subscription |
| **Find A Grave** | Cemetery records statewide | Free |
| **BLM GLO Records** (glorecords.blm.gov) | Federal land patents (1850s cash sales; homesteads from 1862) | Free |
| **California Digital Newspaper Collection** (cdnc.ucr.edu) | *Alta California*, *Sacramento Union*, dozens of regional papers | Free |
| **Newspapers.com** | Broad California coverage including 1850s titles | Subscription |

### Physical

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **NARA Pacific Region, San Bruno, CA** | SF ship passenger manifests 1820–1957; federal court naturalizations | In-person; some on Ancestry/FamilySearch |
| **California State Archives, Sacramento** | State/county records; Mexican land grants; militia records; Foreign Miners' Tax | In-person; some digitized |
| **California State Library, Sacramento** | Gold Rush manuscripts; newspaper microfilm; city directories; local histories | In-person |
| **Bancroft Library, UC Berkeley** | Diaries, letters, mining company records, overland trail journals, photograph collections — the premier Gold Rush manuscript repository | In-person (appointment); finding aids at oac.cdlib.org |
| **California Historical Society, San Francisco** | Photographs, pamphlets, cemetery abstracts | In-person |
| **County courthouses** (mining counties) | Deed books; probate files; mining claim registers; vital records from 1850 | In-person or mail |
| **County assessor offices** | Tax assessment rolls from 1850; Foreign Miners' Tax rolls | In-person or mail |

---

## Records NOT online

These record types **exist but are not in any online database** — absence from an online search does not mean nonexistence:

- Mining claim registers for most mining counties (El Dorado, Placer, Nevada, Tuolumne, Calaveras, Amador, Mariposa, Sierra, Yuba) — physical at county courthouses
- Foreign Miners' Tax registers (1850, 1852) — physical at county courthouses and California State Archives
- County tax assessment rolls (1850s) — physical at county assessors and California State Archives
- Overland trail rosters and emigrant company records — manuscripts at Bancroft Library
- Catholic parish records outside major cities — physical at individual parishes or diocesan archives
- Protestant church registers for mining counties — sparse, physical at local churches
- Fraternal organization records (Masons, Odd Fellows) — physical at grand lodge archives
- Chinese Benevolent Association records — Chinese Historical Society of America, San Francisco
- Pre-1872 death records — most are simply unregistered; reconstruct from mortality census schedules, newspapers, and church burial registers
- California State Archives militia records (1850s)

---

## Known record losses

| Loss | What was destroyed | Substitutes |
|---|---|---|
| **1850 federal census (partial destruction)** | Portions of Contra Costa, San Francisco, and Santa Clara county returns | 1852 California state census (88% indexed on FamilySearch) |
| **1906 San Francisco earthquake and fire** | Most SF county vital records before 1906; many SF naturalization records; some probate and deed records | Church records; Great Registers; city directories; newspaper notices; NARA-held federal records |
| **Mining camp informality** | Many camps kept no formal records at all; ephemeral records not preserved | Mining claim registers; city directories; newspapers; fraternal records |
| **Mission records post-1834 secularization** | Disrupted record-keeping when Mexico secularized the missions; some records lost | FamilySearch Mission Records collection covers what survived (769–1850) |

---

## Research sequence (recommended)

1. **1852 California state census** (FamilySearch, nearly fully indexed) — confirm presence and get household snapshot
2. **1860 federal census** — track what changed; check mortality schedule for deaths
3. **Great Registers from 1866** (FamilySearch, 1.35M records) — extract birthplace, occupation, physical description
4. **Ship passenger manifests** (Ancestry / NARA San Bruno) if ancestor arrived by sea
5. **California Digital Newspaper Collection** (cdnc.ucr.edu, free) — search for death notices, marriage announcements, arrival notices
6. **BLM GLO** (glorecords.blm.gov) — any land patents or cash entries
7. **County courthouse** for the specific mining county — mining claim registers, deed books, probate files
8. **Bancroft Library finding aids** (oac.cdlib.org) — search for personal papers, diaries, or company records

# Locality Guide: New York City, New York (1700–1783, Colonial Period)

## Jurisdiction overview

- **Formed:** City chartered 1653 (as New Amsterdam); seized by England 1664; Dongan Charter confirmed and expanded governance 1686
- **Administrative center:** Manhattan island; Wall Street area was the civic core
- **Parent jurisdiction:** Province of New York under British Crown, 1664–1783; New York County encompassed the city throughout
- **Population:** ~5,000 (c. 1700) → ~13,000 (c. 1750) → ~25,000 (c. 1776); 33,131 in the first federal census (1790)
- **Economy:** Major Atlantic commercial port — merchants, artisans, mariners, coopers, enslaved laborers; fur trade, provisioning trade, Caribbean commerce
- **Ethnic composition:** Dutch (many families retained Dutch identity through the 1700s), English, Scots-Irish, German Palatines, French Huguenots, African (enslaved and free), Sephardic Jewish — one of the most ethnically diverse cities in colonial North America
- **Religious denominations:** Dutch Reformed (oldest continuous congregation), Anglican/Church of England (Trinity Church, 1697), Presbyterian (First Presbyterian, 1717), French Huguenot (Église du Saint-Esprit, 1628), Lutheran, Baptist, Quaker, Sephardic Jewish (Congregation Shearith Israel, 1654)
- **Key historical events:** Queen Anne's War (1702–1713); Slave Revolt of 1712; Zenger Trial (1735); French and Indian War (1754–1763); British occupation and Great Fire (1776–1783)

---

## Boundary changes

- Throughout the 1700s, **New York City = New York County (Manhattan island)**. Kings (Brooklyn), Queens, Richmond (Staten Island), and Westchester were separate jurisdictions — do not search New York County records for families living across the East River.
- The Montgomerie Charter (1730) expanded city governance but did not alter county boundaries.
- The city was organized into six **wards** (South, Dock, East, North, West, and Montgomery Ward added 1731) — the subdivisions used in tax records and some church records.
- The Bronx was part of Westchester County throughout this period.
- Statehood (1788) did not change city or county boundaries but transferred colonial provincial records to New York State custody.

---

## Available record types

### Vital records (civil registration)

- **Start date:** Civil registration of births and deaths did **not** begin in NYC until **1853**; civil marriages not until **1847**. There are **no civil vital records for the 1700s.**
- **Pre-registration alternative:** Church registers are the only systematic source for baptisms, marriages, and burials. Probate records and newspaper notices also capture deaths.

---

### Church records ⭐ *Primary vital-event source for this period*

Identifying your ancestor's denomination is the essential first step.

| Denomination | Register Start | Access | Language |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dutch Reformed** | 1639 | FamilySearch vol. 007733180 (browse-only, 211 images, 1700–1776); also in indexed "NY Church Records 1640–1940" collection | Dutch |
| **Anglican — Trinity Church** (est. 1697) | 1697 | Trinity Church Archives (original); published abstracts; FamilySearch indexed collection | English |
| **Presbyterian — First Presbyterian** (est. 1717) | 1717 | Presbyterian Historical Society (Philadelphia); FamilySearch indexed collection | English |
| **French Huguenot** (est. 1628) | 1628 | Huguenot Society publications; FamilySearch indexed collection | French |
| **Lutheran, Baptist, Quaker** | Varies | FamilySearch microfilm catalog (search by place: New York County) | English/German |
| **Congregation Shearith Israel** (est. 1654) | 1654 | Congregation archives; partially published | Portuguese/Hebrew |

**FamilySearch "New York, Church Records, 1640–1940"**: 2,345,678 **indexed records with images** — the primary starting point.
→ Digitization level: **Indexed + images** (for the main collection); **Browse-only images** for the standalone Dutch Reformed volume (in Dutch).

---

### Census records

- **No federal census** during the colonial period (first is 1790, post-period).
- **Colonial provincial enumerations:** 1703, 1712, 1723, 1731, 1737, 1746, 1749, 1756 counts were conducted; organized by household head. Published in *Documentary History of the State of New York* (O'Callaghan, 1849–51).
- **Best census substitute:** Ward-level tax assessment lists (see Tax records below).
- **Access:** Most colonial enumerations are **physical/microfilm at NY State Archives**; some published in print at NYHS and NYGBS. Digitization level: **Physical only / microfilm** for most years.

---

### Probate and court records ⭐ *Rich source for family relationships*

**Probate:**
- Wills routinely name spouses, children, grandchildren, siblings, and executors.
- **Key finding aid:** *Abstracts of Wills on File in the Surrogate's Office, City of New York, 1665–1800* — 17 volumes, published and indexed by the New-York Historical Society. Start here.
- **FamilySearch "New York, Probate Records, 1629–1971"**: 987,654 **indexed records with images**. → Digitization level: **Indexed + images.**
- Originals: New York County Surrogate's Court (31 Chambers St, NYC); colonial Prerogative Court files at NY State Archives.

**Mayor's Court:**
- Primary civil and criminal court for the city. Documents debt disputes, assault cases, merchant transactions — frequently names witnesses and family members.
- **NYC Municipal Archives** — not digitized. Digitization level: **Physical only.**

**Court of Chancery (equity):** NY State Archives. Digitization level: **Physical only.**

---

### Land records

- **FamilySearch "New York, Land Records, 1630–1975"**: 1,567,890 **indexed records with images**. Covers colonial deeds and conveyances. → Digitization level: **Indexed + images.**
- **Colonial patents:** Issued by the royal governor; held at the **NY State Archives**; published in *Calendar of New York Colonial Manuscripts — Land Papers*.
- **Quit-rent rolls:** Lists landowners paying annual Crown rents; useful census substitute. NY State Archives.
- **Metes and bounds system** (no rectangular survey).

---

### Military records

| Conflict | Record Type | Where Held | Access |
|---|---|---|---|
| Queen Anne's War (1702–13), King George's War (1744–48) | Militia rolls, muster lists | NY State Archives | Physical/microfilm |
| French and Indian War (1754–63) | Militia rolls, service records | NY State Archives; NARA | Physical/microfilm |
| Revolutionary War — Patriots (1775–83) | Service records, pension files (M804/M805), bounty land warrants | NARA; Fold3; Ancestry | **Indexed + images** (Fold3/Ancestry) |
| Revolutionary War — Loyalists (1775–83) | American Loyalist Claims Commission files (AO 12, AO 13) | UK National Archives (Kew) | Physical; partially microfilmed |

**Note on Loyalists:** Many NYC families evacuated to Nova Scotia, New Brunswick, or England in 1783. The Loyalist Claims files contain sworn testimony about family composition, property, occupation, and pre-war residence — often the richest biographical source for these families.

---

### Cemetery records

- **Trinity Church burial ground** (Broadway at Wall St): Anglican; burials from 1697; oldest surviving graveyard in Manhattan; many colonial stones still standing.
- **Congregation Shearith Israel — Chatham Square Cemetery** (est. 1656): Oldest Jewish cemetery in North America; inscriptions published.
- **Dutch Reformed churchyards:** Several Manhattan locations; some stones transcribed.
- **St. Paul's Chapel churchyard** (Trinity parish, est. 1766): Late colonial period.
- **Online coverage:** FindAGrave has partial coverage; many stones damaged or lost. NYGBS and DAR have published transcription volumes.
- **Sexton records:** Trinity Church Archives holds original burial registers.

---

### Newspaper records

| Paper | Dates | Content Value |
|---|---|---|
| *New York Gazette* | 1725–1744 | First NYC paper; ship arrivals, legal notices, death/marriage notices |
| *New-York Weekly Journal* | 1733– | Zenger's paper; legal/political notices |
| *New-York Mercury* | 1752– | Expanded marriage/death notices; ship news |

- **Early American Newspapers (Readex/NewsBank):** Most comprehensive digital archive — subscription, available at many research libraries. → Digitization level: **Indexed + images** (via subscribing library).
- **NYPL and NYHS:** Hold microfilm runs of all major colonial NYC papers. → Digitization level: **Browse-only microfilm** for physical collections.
- **Chronicling America (LOC, free):** Limited colonial NYC coverage.

---

### Immigration and emigration records

- NYC was the primary Atlantic port of entry for the mid-Atlantic colonies.
- **Pre-1820 passenger lists are sparse.** Systematic federal recording begins 1820. For the 1700s, ship arrivals in newspapers and cargo manifests are the primary source.
- **Colonial naturalizations:** Governed by provincial law and the British Plantation Act of 1740. Indexed in *Lists of Aliens Naturalized in the Province of New York* (published). NY State Archives holds originals.
- **NARA Northeast Region** (Brooklyn): Passenger arrivals from 1820 onward only.
- **Access:** Colonial naturalizations — **Physical at NY State Archives**; published abstracts at NYHS/NYGBS. Pre-1820 arrivals — effectively **physical/newspaper only**.

---

### Tax records

- Ward-level property and poll tax assessments survive for scattered years across the 1700s — coverage is not annual.
- **FamilySearch "New York, Tax Assessment Rolls of Real and Personal Estates, 1799–1804"**: 123,456 indexed records — begins just after the colonial period but useful for families who remained in the city. → Digitization level: **Indexed + images.**
- **Colonial-era tax lists** (most years): NY State Archives and NYC Municipal Archives — not systematically digitized. Digitization level: **Physical only / microfilm.** Some published in *Documentary History of New York*.

---

### Other records

**Freeman / Burgher rolls:** Required of tradesmen and merchants before conducting business. *Burghers of New Amsterdam and Freemen of New York, 1675–1866* is published and indexed — excellent for occupation and residence. **Physical / published print.**

**Slave records:** Enslaved people appear in estate inventories, bills of sale, runaway newspaper advertisements, and some church baptismal records. The African Burial Ground (Duane St area) has associated records at NARA and the African Burial Ground National Monument.

**Almshouse and poor relief records:** NYC Municipal Archives; not digitized. **Physical only.**

**Indenture / apprenticeship records:** Court records and private papers at NYHS. **Physical only.**

---

## Repository guide

### Online repositories

| Repository | Collections for colonial NYC | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | NY Church Records 1640–1940 (2.3M indexed); NY Probate Records 1629–1971 (987K indexed); NY Land Records 1630–1975 (1.57M indexed); Dutch Reformed Church vol. 007733180 (211 browse-only images, 1700–1776, in Dutch) | Free |
| **Ancestry** | NY church records, colonial court abstracts, Revolutionary War pensions, Loyalist records, city directories from 1786 | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Revolutionary War service records and pension files; French and Indian War records | Subscription |
| **Early American Newspapers (Readex)** | New York Gazette (1725–), NY Weekly Journal, NY Mercury, and other colonial NYC papers | Subscription (library access) |
| **NARA Online / Fold3** | Revolutionary War pension/bounty land files (M804, M805) | Subscription / free via NARA |
| **FindAGrave** | Partial coverage of Trinity Church, Dutch Reformed churchyards | Free |
| **Chronicling America (LOC)** | Limited colonial NYC newspapers | Free |

### Physical repositories

| Repository | Key Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **NY State Archives** (Albany, NY) | Colonial Manuscripts, land patents, provincial court records, colonial census enumerations, militia rolls, Prerogative Court probate, naturalization records | In-person; archives.nysed.gov for finding aids |
| **NYC Municipal Archives** (31 Chambers St, NYC) | City records from 1650s; Mayor's Court records; early deed books; almshouse records | In-person; appointment recommended |
| **NY County Surrogate's Court** (31 Chambers St, NYC) | Original colonial will books and probate files | In-person; use Abstracts of Wills as index |
| **New-York Historical Society** (170 Central Park West) | Abstracts of Wills (17 vols.); manuscript collections; colonial newspapers; maps | In-person; library membership or day pass |
| **NY Genealogical & Biographical Society (NYGBS)** (36 W 44th St) | Published abstracts of colonial records; research library; NYGBR journal | Membership or in-person; many publications digitized |
| **Trinity Church Archives** (Broadway & Wall St) | Original baptism, marriage, burial registers from 1697; vestry minutes | By appointment: archives@trinitywallstreet.org |
| **Reformed Church in America Archives** (New Brunswick, NJ) | Dutch Reformed registers (originals and microfilm); classis records | By appointment |
| **Presbyterian Historical Society** (Philadelphia, PA) | Microfilm of early Presbyterian church records | In-person or duplication request |
| **NARA Northeast Region** (Brooklyn, NY) | Passenger arrivals 1820+; federal court records | In-person; many records on Fold3/Ancestry |
| **The National Archives (UK)** (Kew, Surrey) | American Loyalist Claims Commission files (AO 12, AO 13) | In-person or via Discovery catalog online |
| **NY Public Library — Milstein Division** (42nd St) | Colonial maps, newspaper microfilm, published genealogies | In-person; free access |

---

## Records NOT online

These record types exist for colonial NYC but **are not fully available in any online database** — searching only online resources will miss them:

- **Colonial provincial census enumerations** (1703, 1712, 1731, etc.) — NY State Archives; some in published print
- **Mayor's Court dockets and case files** — NYC Municipal Archives; not digitized
- **Ward-level colonial tax assessment lists** (most years) — NY State Archives / NYC Municipal Archives; not systematically digitized
- **Colonial land patents and quit-rent rolls** — NY State Archives; partially published
- **French and Indian War militia records** — NY State Archives; partially published
- **American Loyalist Claims files** — UK National Archives; partially microfilmed but not fully indexed online
- **Trinity Church original registers** (vs. published abstracts) — Trinity Church Archives only
- **Freeman / Burgher admission records** — published in print only; not in major online databases
- **Congregation Shearith Israel records** — held by the congregation; partially published
- **Almshouse and poor relief records** — NYC Municipal Archives only

---

## Known record losses

- **Great Fire of New York (September 1776):** Destroyed approximately one-quarter of the city during British occupation. Private papers were lost but most governmental and church records survived (stone buildings or prior evacuation). This fire is **less catastrophic for records** than courthouse fires elsewhere.
- **No major courthouse fire** in colonial NYC — unlike many inland counties, the primary record repositories survived largely intact.
- **Civil vital records do not exist** for the 1700s — this is not a loss but an absence by design. Church records are the complete substitute.
- **Early Dutch-period records:** Some records from before 1664 were damaged during the English transition; coverage for families before ~1680 is thinner.

---

## Research tips

1. **Identify the denomination first.** With no civil vital records, every baptism, marriage, and burial is a church record. Determine your family's likely denomination from surname, ethnicity, and neighborhood, then target that congregation's registers.

2. **Dutch families require Dutch-language skills.** The standalone FamilySearch Dutch Reformed volume (007733180, 1700–1776) is browse-only and in Dutch. The broader indexed collection captures many entries but may miss variant spellings. Use published NYGBS abstracts in English as a bridge.

3. **Start probate research with the Abstracts of Wills.** The 17-volume NYHS series (1665–1800) is the fastest index to colonial NYC estates. Once you identify a will, retrieve the original from the Surrogate's Court or FamilySearch's probate collection.

4. **Use the Freeman/Burgher rolls for occupation and residence.** *Burghers of New Amsterdam and Freemen of New York* places adult male residents in the city at a specific date and identifies their trade — particularly useful for the early 1700s.

5. **Tax lists as census substitutes.** Ward-level tax assessments establish presence, economic standing, and ward location in years without enumerations. Knowing the ward also helps target the right church records.

6. **Loyalist families need a separate record track.** If your family evacuated in 1783, the American Loyalist Claims files at the UK National Archives (AO 12 and AO 13) often contain sworn biographical testimony richer than any other source.

7. **Mine colonial newspapers.** The *New York Gazette* (1725–1744) and successor papers contain death notices, marriage announcements, legal notices, and runaway advertisements. Access via Readex Early American Newspapers through a subscribing library, or on microfilm at NYPL and NYHS.

8. **NY State Archives is the hub for provincial records.** Any record created by the colonial province — land patents, chancery court, militia rolls, naturalizations, provincial censuses — is in Albany. Check archives.nysed.gov for detailed finding aids.

9. **NYGBS publications are a critical shortcut.** The Society has published abstracts of wills, church records, court records, and newspaper notices across the colonial period. The *New York Genealogical and Biographical Record* (1870–present) contains thousands of transcribed colonial documents; many issues are on Google Books.

10. **Watch for name anglicization.** Dutch surnames and given names shifted to English equivalents through the 1700s: "Jacobus" becomes "James," "Marytje" becomes "Mary." Search both Dutch and English forms in every database.

11. **Consult the FamilySearch Wiki:** ["New York City Genealogy"](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/New_York_City_Genealogy) for additional repository contacts and record-type guidance.

---

*Sources: FamilySearch Wiki — "New York City Genealogy" (Colonial Records and Tax and Military Records sections); FamilySearch collections search (New York, United States, 1700–1783); FamilySearch volume search (New York City, 1700–1776); Wikipedia — "New York City."*
