# Locality Guide: Pennsylvania Quaker Communities (c. 1750–1810)

## Jurisdiction overview

Pennsylvania was founded by William Penn in 1681 as an explicitly Quaker colony. By the late 1700s, Quakers were most densely concentrated in the southeastern counties — **Philadelphia, Chester, Bucks, and Delaware** — with significant communities also in Lancaster, York, Montgomery (formed 1784 from Philadelphia County), and Berks counties. The Philadelphia Yearly Meeting governed Quaker affairs across Pennsylvania, New Jersey, and Delaware.

**Critical civil registration fact:** Pennsylvania did **not** require statewide civil registration of births or deaths until **January 1, 1906**. For the late 1700s, **Quaker meeting records are the primary — often only — source for birth, marriage, and death events.** This makes meeting records far more important here than in states with earlier civil registration.

---

## Boundary changes you must track

County boundaries shifted significantly in this period, and records follow the jurisdiction that created them:

| Change | Date | Research implication |
|---|---|---|
| Delaware County carved from Chester County | 1789 | Events before 1789 in Delaware Co. territory → look in Chester Co. records |
| Montgomery County carved from Philadelphia County | 1784 | Gwynedd, Abington meetings shifted county affiliation |
| Lancaster Co. formed from Chester Co. | 1729 | Earlier Lancaster events → Chester Co. records |
| York / Adams Co. formation | 1749–1800 | Check parent county for early records |

**Always identify the county at the date of the event**, not the modern county.

---

## The core record category: Quaker Meeting Records

This is the most important section of this guide. Quaker meetings kept records of exceptional detail and continuity.

### Meeting hierarchy

The **monthly meeting** is the record-creating unit for genealogy. Every Quaker birth, death, marriage, removal, and disciplinary action was recorded there.

| Level | Frequency | What it recorded |
|---|---|---|
| **Monthly meeting** | Monthly | Births, deaths, marriages, removals, disownments, sufferings |
| **Quarterly meeting** | Quarterly | Oversight of monthly meetings; appeals |
| **Yearly meeting** | Annual | Policy epistles; major minutes (Philadelphia Yearly Meeting) |

### Record types within monthly meeting registers

| Record type | Contents | Genealogical value |
|---|---|---|
| **Birth / death registers** | Name, numbered-month date, parents | Direct vital evidence — the civil registration substitute |
| **Marriage certificates** | Bride, groom, date, place, **all attendees' signatures** (30–100+ names) | One document can name an entire family network |
| **Certificates of removal** | Member name; origin meeting; destination meeting | Migration tracker — follows families across moves |
| **Disownment records** | Name; offense (military service, out-marriage, etc.) | Identifies Revolutionary War soldiers; places person at a meeting on a date |
| **Sufferings records** | Fines and distraints for militia refusal | Identifies pacifist members; dates of residence |
| **Meeting minutes** | Ongoing business; birth/death reports | Contextual evidence; sometimes names relatives |

### ⚠️ Quaker date format — required reading

Quakers rejected pagan month names and used **numbered months**. The catch: the calendar year changed in 1752.

- **Before 1752 calendar reform:** 1st Month = **March**; 12th Month = **February** (Old Style year beginning March 25)
- **After 1752:** 1st Month = **January**; 12th Month = **December**

Records from 1750–1755 require care — meetings transitioned at different times. A "1st Month 1750" entry could be March 1750 or January 1750 depending on the meeting's calendar. Double-dated years (e.g., "2 mo 14, 1749/50") appear near the transition.

### Major monthly meetings, late 1700s Pennsylvania

**Philadelphia County / City:** Philadelphia MM (1681), Abington MM, Gwynedd MM (→Montgomery Co. after 1784)

**Chester County:** Chester MM, Goshen MM, Bradford MM, Sadsbury MM, Caln MM, New Garden MM

**Bucks County:** Falls MM, Buckingham MM, Wrightstown MM, Middletown MM, Richland MM

**Delaware County (Chester Co. before 1789):** Middletown MM, Newtown/Chester MM

**Lancaster County:** Caln MM, Little Britain MM, Lancaster MM

**York / Adams County:** Warrington MM, York MM

---

## Where Quaker records are held

### Physical repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Friends Historical Library, Swarthmore College** (Swarthmore, PA) | **Largest collection of American Quaker records.** Originals or copies of minutes, birth/death/marriage registers, removal certificates, and sufferings records for most Pennsylvania monthly meetings | In-person by appointment; catalog at swarthmore.edu/friends; many records also on FamilySearch microfilm |
| **Quaker and Special Collections, Haverford College** (Haverford, PA) | Significant manuscript holdings; Hinshaw research files | In-person by appointment |
| **Historical Society of Pennsylvania (HSP)** (Philadelphia) | Philadelphia Monthly Meeting records; colonial Philadelphia documents | In-person; some digitized |
| **Pennsylvania State Archives** (Harrisburg) | Proprietary land records; colonial court records; Revolutionary War records | In-person; some online at phmc.pa.gov |
| **County Recorder of Deeds / Register of Wills** (each county) | Deed books; probate records | In-person or mail; some on FamilySearch microfilm |

---

## Published abstracts — start here before the originals

**William Wade Hinshaw, *Encyclopedia of American Quaker Genealogy*, 6 volumes (Ann Arbor: Edwards Brothers, 1936–1950)** is the essential finding aid. Volumes 1 and 2 abstract birth, death, and marriage records from dozens of Pennsylvania monthly meetings.

- **FamilySearch:** Digitized and free
- **Ancestry.com:** Digitized, subscription
- **In print:** Major genealogical libraries

> *Important:* Hinshaw abstracts are transcriptions. Always verify against the original meeting register. He occasionally abbreviated names, omitted witnesses, or misread dates.

---

## FamilySearch collections (online)

| Collection | Records | Images | Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| *Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985* | 1,245,830 | 607,220 | **Indexed + images** — includes microfilmed Quaker registers for many meetings |
| *Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994* | 893,214 | 446,607 | **Indexed + images** |
| *Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950* | 312,045 | 156,023 | **Indexed + images** (mostly civil licenses; Quaker marriages underrepresented) |
| *Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900* | 198,765 | 0 | **Index only** — no images |
| Philadelphia Yearly Meeting Minutes, 1750–1800 (image group 001188322) | — | 145 | **Browse-only; 0% indexed; not full-text searchable** |

Beyond these, many individual monthly meeting microfilm reels on FamilySearch are browse-only. Search the **FamilySearch Catalog** by meeting name (e.g., "Goshen Monthly Meeting") to locate them.

---

## Other record types

### Census and census substitutes

- **1790, 1800, 1800 Federal Censuses** — Pennsylvania records survive; heads of household only; **indexed + images** on FamilySearch and Ancestry.
- **No Pennsylvania state census** existed.
- **Pre-1790 substitute: Pennsylvania tax assessment lists** — the primary population record before the first federal census.

### Tax records

| Record | Date range | Access |
|---|---|---|
| Chester County tax lists | From 1693 | FamilySearch microfilm; Chester Co. Archives |
| Pennsylvania Effective Supply Tax | 1779–1781 | Ancestry (partially indexed); FamilySearch |
| *Pennsylvania, Tax Lists, 1762–1794* | 1762–1794 | **Indexed + images** on Ancestry |
| County assessment lists | Vary by county | FamilySearch microfilm; county archives |

### Land records

Pennsylvania is a **state-land state** (Penn proprietors, then Commonwealth — not federal public domain). Two layers:
1. **Warrants, surveys, patents** — Pennsylvania Land Office / State Archives, Harrisburg. Partially digitized.
2. **County deed books** — County Recorder of Deeds from formation date. Largely microfilm or physical; some on FamilySearch.

Quakers were prominent landowners. Deed witnesses and grantees frequently shared meeting membership — useful for community reconstruction.

### Probate records

Wills, administrations, inventories, and guardianships at the county **Register of Wills** from county formation. FamilySearch collection *Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994* (893K indexed records with images) is a strong starting point. Estate distributions often name all children with spouses — critical when vital records are absent.

### Military records — the Revolutionary War dimension

Pennsylvania Quakers faced mass disownment during the Revolution (1775–1785) for:
- Militia or Continental Army service
- Paying militia fines or providing substitutes
- Taking loyalty oaths to Pennsylvania

**Disownment records in meeting minutes are genealogically rich** — they name the person and offense. After disownment, trace the person in civil records.

For those who did serve:
- *Pennsylvania Archives* (Series 2, 5, 6) — muster rolls and supply records; available at major libraries
- **Fold3.com** — Revolutionary War pension files (NARA Series M804); subscription
- **NARA** — Continental service and pension records
- **DAR/SAR lineage applications** — can confirm service and sometimes supply additional genealogical data

A splinter group, the **Free Quakers** (Fighting Quakers, ca. 1781–1836), formed their own meeting in Philadelphia. Their records are at the Free Quaker Meeting House and are less comprehensively microfilmed.

### Cemetery records

Quakers used **plain graves**, often with minimal or no markers, especially before 1800. Meeting burial registers are more reliable than headstones. Major burial grounds are attached to each meetinghouse. **FindAGrave.com** coverage of 18th-century Quaker graves is sparse — use the burial register, not FindAGrave, as your primary source.

### Newspapers

- ***Pennsylvania Gazette*** (Philadelphia, 1728–1800) — the leading colonial paper; carries marriage and death notices, legal/estate notices. Digitized and searchable via Accessible Archives (subscription) and partially on Newspapers.com.
- ***Pennsylvania Packet*, *Freeman's Journal*, *Pennsylvania Journal*** — other Philadelphia papers with partial digitization on Newspapers.com and GenealogyBank.

---

## Records NOT online (important — do not assume nonexistence)

1. **Monthly meeting minutes** for most Pennsylvania meetings (1750–1810) — FamilySearch has microfilm, but most reels are **browse-only** with no name index.
2. **Certificates of removal** — filed within meeting minutes; no separate online index.
3. **Sufferings records** — held at Friends Historical Library; not online.
4. **Philadelphia Yearly Meeting Minutes, 1750–1800** — 145 digitized images, **browse-only, zero percent indexed, not full-text searchable** (FamilySearch image group 001188322).
5. **County deed books** (most counties, pre-1800) — FamilySearch microfilm; little online name indexing.
6. **Orphans' Court records** — mostly physical or unindexed microfilm.
7. **Pennsylvania Land Office warrant/survey records** — partially digitized; State Archives visit may be required.

---

## Research tips

**1. Start with Hinshaw, then go to originals.**
Check the *Encyclopedia of American Quaker Genealogy* (free on FamilySearch) first. If your ancestor appears, it identifies the specific meeting — then retrieve the original register for the full entry, especially the witness list on marriage certificates.

**2. Identify the monthly meeting, not just the county.**
The monthly meeting is the record unit. Two neighbors in the same township might belong to different meetings (one Quaker, one Lutheran). Once you've confirmed Quaker affiliation, determine *which meeting* they attended.

**3. Mine the marriage certificate witness list.**
Every person who attended a Quaker marriage signed the certificate — typically 30 to 100+ signatures. Transcribe every name. This single document can reconstruct an extended family and community network that would take years to assemble from other sources.

**4. Follow removal certificates as a migration tracker.**
When a Quaker moved, they got a removal certificate from their home meeting to carry to the new one. The receiving meeting recorded it in their minutes. If an ancestor vanishes from one meeting's records, search incoming removals at meetings in the likely destination area.

**5. Disownments record the Revolutionary War.**
Members disowned for military service in 1775–1785 are often identifiable in Pennsylvania militia or Continental records. The disownment record and the military record together triangulate the person. Some were later reinstated — check for reinstatement records.

**6. Use tax lists before 1790.**
For the decades before the first federal census, Pennsylvania county tax assessment lists (and the 1779–1781 Effective Supply Tax statewide) are the primary population record. They establish residence and property by township.

**7. Search the FamilySearch Catalog by meeting name.**
Beyond the main indexed collections, search catalog.familysearch.org by the specific meeting name (e.g., "Goshen Monthly Meeting," "Buckingham Monthly Meeting") to find microfilmed records not included in broader collections. Many exist as browse-only reels accessible via the FamilySearch image viewer.

**8. Watch the calendar.**
All Quaker records use numbered months. Pre-1752: 1st Month = March. Post-1752: 1st Month = January. For events 1750–1755, verify which system the meeting was using before committing to a date.

**9. Consult the FamilySearch Wiki article on Pennsylvania Church Records.**
The article at https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Pennsylvania_Church_Records provides a meeting-by-meeting survey of what has been microfilmed and where records are held — the best single orientation before visiting Friends Historical Library.

---

*Guide written to: `/pennsylvania-quaker-communities-locality-guide.md`*
*Sources: FamilySearch Wiki (Pennsylvania Church Records; Pennsylvania Vital Records; Pennsylvania Land Records); Wikipedia; FamilySearch MCP tools (collections_search, volume_search, wiki_search, wikipedia_search).*

# Locality Guide: Pittsburgh / Allegheny County, Pennsylvania (1860–1910)
*Focus: Occupational and industrial sources for steel-industry families*

---

## Jurisdiction overview

- **County formed:** 1788 from Washington and Westmoreland Counties; Pittsburgh incorporated 1758, chartered as a city 1816
- **County seat:** Pittsburgh
- **Population:** Exploded from ~49,000 (Pittsburgh, 1860) to 321,000 (1900) to 775,000+ countywide by 1900
- **Economy:** Carnegie Steel, Jones & Laughlin, and dozens of smaller mills lined the Monongahela, Allegheny, and Ohio riverbanks; associated industries in glass, coke, coal, and rail
- **Ethnic composition:** Early Irish and German settlers were joined from the 1870s–1900s by Poles, Slovaks, Croatians, Ukrainians, Italians, Hungarians, and Lithuanians — plus growing African American migration from the South
- **Key event:** The **Homestead Steel Strike (1892)** is documented by name in congressional records and newspapers — a direct genealogical source

> **Important boundary note:** The independent city of **Allegheny** (across the Ohio River) was a separate municipality until **annexed by Pittsburgh in 1907**. Records for Allegheny City residents before 1907 are in Allegheny City record sets, not Pittsburgh's. Mill towns — Homestead, Braddock, McKeesport, Duquesne — are separate Allegheny County boroughs with their own municipal and parish records.

---

## Available record types

### Vital records (civil registration)

| Record | Date range | Status |
|---|---|---|
| County births & deaths | 1870–1905 | Microfilm / physical — PA State Archives; partial Ancestry index |
| Statewide death certificates | 1906–1968 | **Indexed + images** — FamilySearch (4.2M records), Ancestry |
| Marriage licenses | 1885–present | **Indexed + images** — FamilySearch (*PA County Marriages 1885–1950*, 524K records); Allegheny County courthouse |
| Earlier marriages | 1677–1885 | **Indexed + images** — FamilySearch (*PA Civil Marriages 1677–1950*, 312K; *PA Compiled Marriages 1700–1900*, 199K index-only) |

Pennsylvania **did not mandate statewide birth and death registration until 1906.** Allegheny County registered locally from 1870, but coverage is incomplete — many immigrant births appear only in church baptismal registers.

---

### Church records *(highest-value source for 1860–1906)*

Church registers are the **primary vital record** for the steel-era gap before 1906. Immigrant communities organized parishes by ethnic group — getting the right parish is often the critical research step.

- **Roman Catholic ethnic parishes** (Polish, Slovak, Croatian, Italian, Irish): Baptism, marriage, and burial registers from the 1840s onward; records may be in Polish, Slovak, Croatian, or Latin. **Catholic Diocese of Pittsburgh Archives** holds originals for closed parishes.
- **German Lutheran / Reformed:** Records often in German; dates from 1840s–1850s.
- **Greek / Byzantine Catholic:** Serves Carpatho-Rusyn and Ukrainian communities; records in Church Slavonic or Ukrainian.
- **Jewish congregations:** Synagogue and burial society (chevra kadisha) records at the **Rauh Jewish History Program, Heinz History Center.**
- **African American Baptist / AME:** Held by individual congregations; survival is variable.

**FamilySearch** has *Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985* — **1.2 million records, indexed + images.** Many Allegheny County ethnic Catholic parishes are *not* in this collection — contact the Diocese Archives directly.

---

### Census records

| Year | Key fields for steel workers | Access |
|---|---|---|
| 1870 | Occupation, real/personal property value | Indexed + images (Ancestry, FamilySearch) |
| 1880 | Occupation, birthplace of parents | **Indexed + images — free on FamilySearch** |
| 1890 | **DESTROYED** — ~99% lost in 1921 fire | Use city directories and tax records instead |
| 1900 | Occupation, birthplace of parents, **immigration year**, years in US, citizenship status | Indexed + images (Ancestry, FamilySearch) |
| 1910 | Occupation, industry, employment status, language spoken | Indexed + images (Ancestry, FamilySearch) |

> Pennsylvania conducted **no statewide census** between federal enumerations — city directories and tax records fill the gaps.

---

### Occupational and industrial records *(specialty focus — largely not online)*

This is the highest-value category unique to Pittsburgh research — and the one most researchers miss because it requires repository contact.

**Company employment records**
Survive fragmentarily. Coverage is uneven by company and year.
- **Senator John Heinz History Center** (Pittsburgh): Employment ledgers, payroll books, and personnel files for some mills. Contact the Library & Archives before visiting.
- **Rivers of Steel National Heritage Area** (Homestead, PA): Focuses on Homestead Works and mill communities along the three rivers; holds photographs, oral histories, and some company documents.
- **Hagley Museum and Library** (Wilmington, DE): Some U.S. Steel corporate-era records.
- **Digitization level: Physical only.**

**Union membership records**
The Amalgamated Association of Iron, Steel and Tin Workers (AA) was the primary union before the 1892 Homestead Strike. Surviving lodge membership rolls and meeting minutes are at the **Heinz History Center** (Historical Society of Western Pennsylvania collection).
- **Digitization level: Physical only.**

**State factory inspector reports**
Pennsylvania's Bureau of Industrial Statistics published annual reports listing factories, locations, and employee counts — useful for confirming an ancestor worked at a specific plant. Not name-indexed.
- **Held at: Pennsylvania State Archives (Harrisburg)**; some university libraries.
- **Digitization level: Physical only.**

**City directories** *(most accessible occupational source)*
Pittsburgh city directories begin in **1813** and run continuously. They list head of household by name, occupation, and street address — updated annually, making them the best tool for the missing 1890 census years and for tracking movement between mill towns.
- **Ancestry:** Many Pittsburgh directory years digitized — **indexed + images.**
- **Internet Archive:** Additional years — **browse-only images.**
- **Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh:** Complete physical run from 1813.

---

### Fraternal and mutual aid society records *(frequently overlooked)*

Immigrant steel workers almost universally joined ethnic fraternal societies that functioned as insurance companies. **These records often survive when company records do not.**

- **Organizations:** Polish National Alliance, Slovak Catholic Sokol, Croatian Fraternal Union (headquartered in Pittsburgh), Sons of Italy, Hungarian fraternal lodges
- **What they contain:** Membership rolls, sick benefit claims, death benefit files — documenting names, ages, addresses, and sometimes hometown of origin in the old country
- **Where held:** National headquarters of surviving organizations; **Heinz History Center** holds deposited collections from some organizations
- **Digitization level: Physical only.** Contact organization headquarters or Heinz History Center.

---

### Naturalization records

Before September 1906, men could naturalize in *any* court — local, state, or federal. After that date, all naturalizations went through federal courts.

- **Allegheny County Court of Common Pleas naturalizations (1798–1930):** At the **National Archives at Philadelphia (NARA Mid-Atlantic Region).** Partial index on Ancestry.
- **Post-1906 federal naturalizations:** USCIS Genealogy Program — written request required; fee charged. **Physical only.**
- **Research tip:** The 1900 and 1910 census columns for "immigration year" and "citizenship status" (Al = alien, Pa = first papers filed, Na = naturalized) guide you to the right court and approximate year.

---

### Immigration / passenger records

- **Primary ports:** New York (Castle Garden to 1892, then Ellis Island); **Baltimore** (B&O Railroad terminus — many Central and Eastern Europeans arrived here and traveled overland to Pittsburgh).
- **Indexed + images:** Ancestry, FamilySearch (post-1820 manifests).
- **Post-1895 manifests** include hometown of origin, US contact person, and physical description — the hometown field is often the key to finding European records.
- **Chain migration tip:** Search the full manifest page around your ancestor — neighbors from the same village often traveled together and sailed on the same ship.

---

### Probate and land records

- **Probate:** Allegheny County Register of Wills, from 1788. FamilySearch *Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994* — **893,000 records, indexed + images.** Inventories name household goods, debts, and multiple family members — valuable even for modest estates.
- **Land / deeds:** Allegheny County Recorder of Deeds (metes and bounds system). Note: many immigrant workers **rented** company housing or boarded — absence from deed records doesn't mean absence from the area. Use tax records instead.
- **Tax assessment records:** Annual county assessments from 1788; the best **1890 census substitute.** Held at Pennsylvania State Archives — **microfilm, not indexed online.**

---

### Military records

| Record | Coverage | Access |
|---|---|---|
| Civil War service & pension files | 1861–1865 | **Indexed + images** — Fold3, Ancestry |
| WWI draft registration cards | Men born ~1872–1900 | **Indexed + images** — Ancestry, FamilySearch; list occupation and employer |
| PA adjutant general / regimental records | Civil War | Microfilm / physical — PA State Archives |
| GAR (Grand Army of Republic) post records | Union veterans | Physical — Heinz History Center, PA State Archives |

> WWI draft cards (1917–1918) are especially useful for men who immigrated in the 1880s–1900s — they list current employer, often naming a specific mill.

---

### Cemetery records

- **Major cemeteries:** Allegheny Cemetery (est. 1844); Calvary Catholic Cemetery; Homewood Cemetery (est. 1878); plus numerous ethnic parish cemeteries in each mill town.
- **FindAGrave / BillionGraves:** Extensive Pittsburgh-area coverage — **indexed.**
- **Sexton registers:** Physical only — contact individual cemeteries or Heinz History Center.

---

### Newspaper records

- **English-language papers:** Pittsburgh Gazette (est. 1786), Pittsburgh Post, Pittsburgh Dispatch. Strong coverage on **Newspapers.com** (subscription); selected titles on **Chronicling America** (free). Microfilm at Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh.
- **Ethnic-language press:** Polish (*Pittsburczanin*), Slovak, Croatian, and other papers published vital notices in the heritage language — Heinz History Center holds some; largely **microfilm or physical only.**
- **High-value content:** Obituaries name survivors, home village, church, and fraternal affiliations. 1892 Homestead Strike coverage names specific workers. Sheriff's sale and estate probate notices appear in papers of record.

---

## Repository guide summary

### Online repositories

| Repository | Key Pittsburgh collections | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | Church records, marriages, death certificates, probate, census 1880/1900/1910 | Free |
| **Ancestry** | Census all years, city directories, passenger lists, Civil War pensions, WWI draft cards, some vitals | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Civil War service & pension files, Spanish-American War | Subscription |
| **Newspapers.com** | Pittsburgh dailies | Subscription |
| **Chronicling America** (LOC) | Selected PA newspapers | Free |
| **FindAGrave** | Cemetery inscriptions | Free |
| **Internet Archive** | Some Pittsburgh city directories (browse-only) | Free |

### Physical repositories

| Repository | Key holdings |
|---|---|
| **Senator John Heinz History Center**, Pittsburgh | Company employment records, union records, fraternal society collections, Pittsburgh Jewish Archives, city directories, oral histories |
| **Rivers of Steel National Heritage Area**, Homestead PA | Homestead Works documentation, mill-town community records, photographs |
| **Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh** (Pennsylvania Dept.) | Complete city directories 1813–present, local history, newspaper microfilm |
| **National Archives at Philadelphia (NARA Mid-Atlantic)** | Allegheny County naturalizations 1798–1930; federal census originals |
| **Catholic Diocese of Pittsburgh Archives** | Parish registers (baptism, marriage, burial) for closed parishes |
| **Pennsylvania State Archives**, Harrisburg | Factory inspector reports, tax assessment records, adjutant general records, state land records |
| **Allegheny County Courthouse** | Marriage licenses 1885+, deeds, court records, probate |

---

## Known record losses

- **1890 federal census: DESTROYED.** Bridge 1880–1900 with Pittsburgh city directories (Ancestry / Carnegie Library), tax assessment records (PA State Archives), and church records.
- **Pre-1870 Allegheny County vital records:** No county-level or statewide registration exists. Church records are the only systematic vital source.
- **Carnegie Steel employment records:** U.S. Steel did not systematically preserve pre-1901 mill employment records. What survives at Heinz History Center is the result of selective donation — absence from surviving records does not mean your ancestor did not work there.
- **No courthouse fire** has affected Allegheny County — court and deed records are generally intact from 1788.

---

## Research strategy for steel-worker families

1. **Census first.** The 1880 and 1900 censuses establish occupation, employer type, birthplace, and (in 1900) immigration year. This drives everything else.

2. **City directories as the 1890 bridge.** Run 1888–1892 directories to document presence and address during the destroyed-census decade.

3. **Identify the ethnic parish before searching church records.** Slovak Catholics → Slovak parishes; Polish Catholics → Polish parishes. The Diocese Archives can map which parish served which neighborhood. Getting the right parish unlocks pre-1906 vital records.

4. **Use the 1900 immigration year to focus passenger list searches.** Narrow the manifest search to a two- or three-year window; then look at the whole manifest page for traveling companions.

5. **Naturalization records before 1906 are scattered.** Search Allegheny County Court of Common Pleas records at NARA Philadelphia first, then broaden to federal district court and neighboring counties.

6. **Fraternal society records substitute for missing company records.** When a death benefit was paid, the lodge recorded age, date of death, wife's name, and sometimes children. Contact the Croatian Fraternal Union (Pittsburgh HQ) or Polish National Alliance (Chicago HQ) directly.

7. **Don't miss the WWI draft card** (even for pre-1900 arrivals). Cards for men born in the 1870s–1880s list current employer and occupation — often naming a specific mill.

8. **Homestead Strike records name workers.** Congressional investigation testimony (1892), Pittsburgh Survey reports (Russell Sage Foundation, 1907–1908; digitized on archive.org), and newspaper coverage provide rare individual-level documentation of mill workers.

9. **Watch for spelling variants.** Slovak, Polish, and Croatian names were anglicized, phonetically respelled, or arbitrarily shortened. Kováč → Kovac / Kovach / Kovack / Kovacs. Search all plausible variants in early naturalization records and directories.

10. **Check adjacent counties.** The steel corridor extended into **Beaver County** (Ambridge, Beaver Falls) and **Washington County** (Monessen, Donora) — workers moved between plants and records may be in neighboring county repositories.

---

## Philadelphia's 1793 Yellow Fever Epidemic: Historical Context for Genealogical Research

### What happened — and why it matters for records

The yellow fever epidemic of 1793 struck Philadelphia from **August through November**, killing approximately **5,000 of the city's roughly 50,000 residents** — one person in ten (Wikipedia: *Yellow fever epidemic of 1793*; FamilySearch Wiki: *Philadelphia Genealogy*). As the nation's capital and largest city, Philadelphia had unusually sophisticated record-keeping for the era, and the scale of the crisis drove officials and private citizens to document deaths, relief efforts, and orphaned children in ways that have real genealogical value today.

Two converging factors shape your research strategy:

1. **Pennsylvania had no statewide civil vital registration until 1906.** Church records are the primary source for births, marriages, and burials throughout this period. The epidemic created a concentrated spike in church burials from August–November 1793 that stands out clearly in surviving registers. *(FamilySearch Wiki: Philadelphia Genealogy — Church Records)*

2. **Thousands fled the city.** Families who evacuated may have left a thin or absent Philadelphia record trail for late 1793 and may appear instead in records from surrounding counties (Montgomery, Bucks, Chester, Delaware) or across the Delaware River in New Jersey. An abrupt gap in Philadelphia records can itself be a clue.

---

### Record categories with particular value for this period

#### 1. Mathew Carey's published death list (1793)
Mathew Carey, a Philadelphia printer, compiled and published *A Short Account of the Malignant Fever Lately Prevalent in Philadelphia* (1793), which includes **named lists of the dead by street and ward**. This is the most widely cited epidemic-specific finding tool. It has been reprinted and is accessible in digitized form through the Internet Archive and through repositories below. *(FamilySearch Wiki: Philadelphia Genealogy — Yellow Fever 1793)*

#### 2. Committee of Philadelphia minutes and reports
Mayor Matthew Clarkson organized a citizens' committee to manage relief, burials, and public order. The committee kept **minutes naming individuals** — people who received relief, people buried at public expense, and officers managing the crisis. These are manuscript sources. *(Wikipedia; FamilySearch Wiki)*

#### 3. Bush Hill hospital records
Bush Hill was converted into an emergency fever hospital. Records from this institution — admissions, deaths, and discharges — document individuals who sought treatment. *(FamilySearch Wiki: Philadelphia Genealogy — Yellow Fever 1793)*

#### 4. Church burial registers — concentrated spike
Every congregation that maintained registers shows a sharp increase in burials from August–November 1793. Churches with well-preserved registers include:
- **Christ Church** (Anglican/Episcopal)
- **Gloria Dei (Old Swedes' Church)** — one of the oldest surviving Philadelphia registers
- **St. Mary's Catholic Church**
- **German Reformed and Lutheran congregations**
- **African Episcopal Church of St. Thomas** and **Bethel AME** — relevant for Black Philadelphians
*(FamilySearch Wiki: Philadelphia Genealogy — Church Records)*

The Genealogical Society of Pennsylvania has compiled indexes to many Philadelphia church records; these are an efficient entry point. *(FamilySearch Wiki)*

#### 5. Orphans' Court and guardianship records
The epidemic produced a surge in orphaned children. Philadelphia's **Orphans' Court** — which handled guardianship, estate administration, and placement of minor children — saw sharply elevated activity in late 1793 and 1794. These records can reveal family structure: who was appointed guardian (usually a relative), how estates were divided, and which children existed. Probate records from this same period often document families that had no prior paper trail. *(General knowledge — not directly confirmed by tool results; verify at repositories below.)*

#### 6. Guardians of the Poor records
The Guardians of the Poor managed public relief and institutional care for Philadelphia's indigent population. Their records document families who received assistance and children placed in the almshouse. *(General knowledge — verify at Philadelphia City Archives.)*

#### 7. Free African Society and African American records
The Free African Society, led by Absalom Jones and Richard Allen, organized Black Philadelphians to nurse the sick and bury the dead when many white residents had fled. This community's service was documented both in the Committee records and in a separate account Jones and Allen published in 1794. For researchers with African American ancestors in Philadelphia, this context matters: **Black Philadelphians appear in epidemic records in disproportionate numbers as caregivers and as victims**, and the Free African Society's own membership and activity records are a distinct source. *(General knowledge — not directly returned by the tool searches; verify at HSP and the Afro-American Historical and Cultural Museum collections.)*

---

### Key repositories (tool-confirmed)

The following repositories are confirmed by Wikipedia and the FamilySearch Wiki as holding primary epidemic-era sources:

| Repository | Core holdings |
|---|---|
| **Historical Society of Pennsylvania (HSP)** | Manuscript Committee minutes, personal papers (Dr. Benjamin Rush's extensive papers and medical accounts), church record transcriptions, Carey's printed account |
| **Philadelphia City Archives** | Municipal records including Guardians of the Poor, Orphans' Court, early death registers |
| **Various church archives** | Original burial registers; many have been microfilmed by FamilySearch |

*The following are based on general knowledge and should be verified directly:*
- **College of Physicians of Philadelphia** — historical medical records and institutional archives from the epidemic period
- **Library Company of Philadelphia** — a major repository for early printed sources including Carey's pamphlet and contemporary newspapers
- **Genealogical Society of Pennsylvania** — compiled church record indexes and transcript collections

---

### Research implications — what to do with this context

| Situation | What to check |
|---|---|
| Ancestor disappears from Philadelphia records in mid-1793 | May have fled — search Montgomery, Bucks, Chester, Delaware counties and Burlington/Gloucester counties NJ for 1793–1794 |
| Ancestor dies with no church burial record | May have been buried at public expense; check Committee records and Carey's list |
| Sudden appearance of orphaned children in records | Orphans' Court and Guardians of the Poor records from 1793–1794 may document surviving family members |
| African American ancestor in Philadelphia ca. 1793 | Free African Society records and African Episcopal/Bethel AME registers; Jones & Allen's 1794 account |
| Estate settled unusually quickly in 1793–1794 | Probate surge was common; the estate file may name all surviving heirs |
| No probate record despite known death | Individual may have died too poor to leave an estate — look for almshouse and Guardians of the Poor records instead |

---

### A note on what this skill covers vs. what locality-guide covers

This response explains **why** certain records exist and **what historical forces shaped them**. For a comprehensive survey of **all** Philadelphia genealogical record types (census, land, naturalization, military, vital records through the full range of dates), what's been microfilmed and indexed, and how to access specific repositories, the **locality-guide** skill would complement this response well. The epidemic context above is the lens for interpreting what you find — locality-guide provides the full map of what survives and where.

## The Great Chicago Fire of 1871: Genealogical Impact and Research Strategy

### What Happened — and Why It Matters for Records

The fire burned October 8–10, 1871, destroying approximately 3.3 square miles of the city and leaving more than 100,000 residents homeless. The most consequential loss for genealogists was the **Cook County Courthouse**, which housed the city's official vital record registers (births, deaths), deed books, probate files, court records, and tax rolls. Many church registers located in the burn zone were also destroyed. *(Wikipedia, "Great Chicago Fire")*

This creates a hard wall in the pre-fire record trail: for ancestors in Chicago before October 1871, you are largely working with federal records, church records from outside the burn zone, and a handful of surrogates.

---

### What Was Destroyed

| Record Type | Status |
|---|---|
| Cook County deed books | Destroyed |
| County birth and death registers | Destroyed |
| Probate files (pre-1871) | Largely destroyed |
| Court records (pre-1871) | Largely destroyed |
| Tax rolls | Destroyed |
| Many Catholic and Protestant parish registers | Destroyed (those within the burn zone) |

---

### What Survived — Your Primary Pre-Fire Sources

**1. Federal Census Schedules (1850, 1860, 1870)**
The originals were held in Washington, D.C., not at the courthouse. All three pre-fire census years for Chicago survived intact. *(FamilySearch Wiki, "Cook County, Illinois Genealogy — Record Loss and the Great Fire")* The **1870 census** — taken just one year before the fire — is your closest pre-fire snapshot of every household. It records names, ages, birthplaces, occupations, and real/personal property values, and it is fully searchable on FamilySearch.

**2. City Directories (1840s onward)**
Pre-fire city directories survived and are held at the **Newberry Library**. These list heads of household by name with occupation and street address — an essential substitute when vital records are gone. They can establish residence, occupational identity, and neighbors (who may be kin).

**3. Church Registers from Outside the Burn Zone**
Parishes on the South Side and West Side were largely untouched by the fire. If your ancestor attended a church in those neighborhoods, its registers may be intact. The fire burned primarily north and northeast from the downtown core.

---

### Substitute and Reconstructed Records — The Heart of Post-Fire Research

**4. Burnt Record Act (1872) — Reconstructed Deeds**
Illinois enacted the Burnt Record Act of 1872, requiring property owners to re-register their deeds to re-establish title. These filings are not originals but are legally reconstructed documents filed after the fact. They name the property owner, legal description, and often chain-of-title information. This is your primary substitute for pre-fire land ownership. *(Wikipedia; FamilySearch Wiki)*

**5. Chicago Title and Trust Company Insurance Records**
Insurance company records — especially those of the **Chicago Title and Trust Company** — became critical substitutes for destroyed property records. This collection is now held at the **Chicago History Museum** and is particularly valuable for tracing property ownership and family economic circumstances before the fire. *(FamilySearch Wiki)*

**6. Post-Fire Vital Records (Cook County Clerk, 1871 onward)**
The Cook County Clerk's office holds vital records from 1871 onward. Note that Illinois did not require statewide civil registration of births and deaths until 1916 — county-level registration was inconsistent throughout the 1870s–1900s. FamilySearch has microfilmed many of these post-fire Cook County records.

**7. The 1880 Federal Census**
The next decennial census after the fire captures families who were present in Chicago in 1880. It includes relationship-to-head-of-household data (new in 1880) and birthplace of parents — connecting your ancestor to their pre-fire origins even when local records are gone.

**8. Chicago Relief and Aid Society Records** *(general knowledge — not tool-confirmed; verify at the Chicago History Museum)*
The Chicago Relief and Aid Society, a private charitable organization, coordinated disaster relief after the fire and reportedly maintained records of displaced families and relief recipients. These records may name family members, addresses, and circumstances. The Chicago History Museum is the most likely repository for this material.

---

### Ethnic Community Records — A Key Strategy for Chicago

Chicago in 1871 was a city of immigrants. The ethnic composition is critical to your research strategy, because immigrant communities maintained **parallel record systems** — ethnic churches, foreign-language newspapers, and mutual aid societies — that often survived when official records did not.

- **German immigrants** (the largest group in 1870s Chicago) had Lutheran and Catholic parish registers, German-language newspapers (*Illinois Staats-Zeitung*), and German mutual aid societies. These records often duplicated vital information.
- **Scandinavian (Norwegian, Swedish) immigrants** maintained Lutheran church registers that frequently recorded family origins in the home country, providing a bridge back to pre-immigration records.
- **Irish Catholic immigrants** are traceable through Catholic parish registers held at the **Archdiocese of Chicago's Joseph Cardinal Bernardin Archives**, which holds surviving Catholic parish registers — including those of parishes re-established after the fire.
- **African American families** may appear in freedmen's records, church records (Baptist, AME), and the records of organizations like the African Methodist Episcopal church.

The key principle: **the ethnic community record often survived when the government record did not.** Identifying your ancestor's church affiliation and ethnic newspaper is as important as identifying their county record.

---

### Key Repositories

| Repository | What It Holds |
|---|---|
| **Newberry Library** (Chicago) | City directories 1840s+, maps, manuscripts, printed local histories |
| **Chicago History Museum** | Chicago Title & Trust insurance records, photographs, personal papers, newspapers |
| **Illinois Regional Archives Depository (IRAD) at Northeastern Illinois University** | Cook County records post-1871 |
| **Archdiocese of Chicago — Joseph Cardinal Bernardin Archives** | Catholic parish registers (surviving and re-established) |
| **Cook County Clerk's Office** | Vital records from 1871 onward |
| **FamilySearch** | Microfilmed post-fire Cook County records; 1850/1860/1870 censuses; military records |
| **National Archives (NARA)** | Federal census originals; Civil War pension files (many Chicago residents were veterans) |

*(Repositories: FamilySearch Wiki, "Cook County, Illinois Genealogy — Repositories")*

---

### Actionable Research Sequence

1. **Start with the 1870 federal census.** It is your most complete pre-fire household snapshot and survived intact. Note neighbors — they are frequently kin or community members.
2. **Check city directories** at the Newberry Library for the 1860s–early 1870s to confirm address, occupation, and proximity to the burn zone.
3. **Identify church affiliation and ethnic community.** This opens a parallel record trail that is independent of the destroyed courthouse records.
4. **Search Burnt Record Act filings** if your ancestor owned property before 1871.
5. **Consult the Chicago Title and Trust collection** at the Chicago History Museum for pre-fire insurance and property records.
6. **Check the 1880 census** for post-fire family reconstitution — many families were displaced after 1871, so the 1880 census may show them in a different neighborhood, suburb, or even a different state if they did not return.
7. **Search Civil War pension files** (NARA) — many Chicago men served in the Union Army. Pension depositions from the 1870s–1890s often contain affidavits from neighbors and kin describing pre-fire circumstances.

---

### A Note on Migration After the Fire

Over 100,000 people were displaced. Many left the city temporarily or permanently, resettling in nearby towns (Evanston, Joliet, Aurora), other Midwestern cities, or even returning to their states of origin. If your ancestor disappears from Chicago records after 1871, **the fire is the most likely explanation** — they may have relocated rather than died. Search city directories and censuses for surrounding Cook County townships and neighboring counties in the early 1870s before concluding they left the Midwest entirely.

---

**Bottom line:** The Great Fire is a genuine record gap — but it is not a dead end. The 1870 census, city directories, ethnic church registers, Burnt Record Act deeds, and insurance records form a surprisingly robust substitute record set when worked together. The fire's destruction of official government records makes the ethnic community and church record trail *more* important for Chicago research than for almost any other major American city of the same era.

# Locality Guide: Louisiana — French Colonial Period (1699–1769)

## Jurisdiction overview

French Louisiana (*La Louisiane*) was governed by the **Superior Council (*Conseil Supérieur*)** — simultaneously court, legislature, and administrative authority — under French rule from 1699 to 1762. New Orleans, founded in 1718 by Bienville, was the capital. Spain assumed actual control in 1769 under Governor O'Reilly. The 1700s therefore spans **two colonial regimes**, and both produced genealogically important records.

The colony had no counties. It was organized into administrative **posts (*postes*)**: Pointe Coupée, Natchitoches, Côte des Allemands (German Coast), Attakapas, Opelousas, etc. **Modern Louisiana parishes did not exist until after statehood in 1812.** Identify which colonial post your ancestor lived in before searching, because records are organized by post, not by modern parish boundaries.

Key population groups: French metropolitan and Canadian-born colonists, German settlers (arriving ~1720s), Acadian exiles (*Cajuns*, 1755–1785), enslaved Africans (primarily from Senegambia and the Bight of Benin), free people of color, and Indigenous nations.

---

## Boundary changes

| Date | Change |
|---|---|
| 1699–1718 | First settlements at Biloxi/Mobile; New Orleans not yet founded |
| 1762 (effective 1769) | France cedes Louisiana to Spain via Treaty of Fontainebleau |
| 1763 | Eastern bank of Mississippi + Mobile/Pensacola go to Britain as British West Florida |
| 1800–1803 | Brief French reacquisition; Louisiana Purchase ends European rule |

Records from the eastern bank (including Mobile) after 1763 will be in British/later American repositories for Alabama and Mississippi, not Louisiana.

---

## Available record types

### 1. Catholic Parish Registers ⭐ *Most important source*
**Digitization: Indexed + images (FamilySearch)**

The Catholic Church was the **only legally recognized religion** in French Louisiana. The *Code Noir* (Black Code) **required Catholic baptism of all enslaved persons**, making parish registers the primary source for both free and enslaved families.

- **Earliest records:** St. Louis Cathedral, New Orleans — from **1720**. Natchitoches from the 1730s. Other posts vary.
- **Record types:** Baptisms, marriages, burials. Marriage entries are exceptionally rich — they routinely name both sets of parents (including whether deceased) and the parents' place of origin.
- **Language:** French (occasionally Latin)
- **FamilySearch collection:** *Louisiana, Church Records, 1718–1925* (collection 1612905) — **~988,000 indexed records with images, free.** This is your first stop.
- **Also held at:** Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives (originals, in-person); Earl K. Long Library at UNO (microfilm)

> **Enslaved ancestors:** Baptismal entries for enslaved persons sometimes record African ethnic or geographic origins (e.g., *"de nation Bambara"*), the enslaver's name, and godparents — often other enslaved or free Black individuals. These are the primary documents for tracing African-descended families in this period.

---

### 2. Superior Council Records
**Digitization: Partially browse-only / physical access**

The Superior Council's records are the colonial equivalent of court, probate, and notarial records combined. Covering roughly 1714–1769.

- **Record types:** Land concession grants, estate inventories (*inventaires après décès*), civil and criminal proceedings, contracts, manumissions (freedom papers), guardianship records
- **Estate inventories** list all household property, debts, and enslaved individuals by name, age, and sometimes origin — irreplaceable for family reconstruction
- **Language:** French; Spanish-era successor records (Cabildo) in Spanish
- **Where held:** **Louisiana Historical Center** (Louisiana State Museum), 400 Esplanade Ave., New Orleans — the main body of records; partially microfilmed by FamilySearch but not fully indexed

---

### 3. Notarial Records
**Digitization: Partially indexed online; largely physical access**

The New Orleans Notarial Archives are the **oldest continuous notarial office in North America**, with acts surviving from the **1730s**. French civil law required contracts, wills, and property transactions to be executed before a notary.

- **Record types:** Marriage contracts (*contrats de mariage*), wills (*testaments*), property sales, manumissions, powers of attorney, business partnerships
- **Language:** French (1730s–1769); Spanish thereafter
- **Where held:** **New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center**, 1340 Poydras St. (notarialarchives.org); open to the public; partial online indexes

---

### 4. Colonial Censuses (*Dénombrements*)
**Digitization: Partially digitized at ANOM (browse); print transcripts**

French administrators conducted periodic population counts. Surviving rolls are scattered.

- **Known censuses:** 1699, 1708, 1721, 1726, 1731, 1739, and others — district coverage varies. Better rolls list individuals by name; others count by household.
- **Where held:**
  - **Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer (ANOM)**, Aix-en-Provence, France (anom.archivesnationales.culture.gouv.fr) — primary repository; online catalog; some documents digitized
  - **Louisiana Historical Center** — transcripts and copies
  - **Published:** Transcripts in *Louisiana Genealogical Register* and *New Orleans Genesis*

---

### 5. Land Records (Concessions and Grants)
**Digitization: American State Papers — fully digitized + searchable (free); ANOM — partial**

French Louisiana used the **long-lot (*arpent*) system** — narrow strips fronting the Mississippi — under French civil law, not English common law.

- **Record types:** Original concession grants, surveys (*arpentages*), notarial sale and inheritance transfers
- **Where held:**
  - **ANOM**, Aix-en-Provence — original concession files
  - **Louisiana State Land Office**, Baton Rouge (doa.la.gov/OLO) — colonial grant confirmations; searchable database, free
  - ***American State Papers: Public Lands*** — printed compilation of post-Purchase colonial grant confirmations, searchable by grantee name; freely available on HathiTrust and Google Books. This is a highly accessible surrogate — Congress required all colonial claimants to re-petition after 1803, often naming the original grantee generations back.
  - **New Orleans Notarial Archives** — transfers and sales

---

### 6. Probate Records
**Digitization: Indexed + images on FamilySearch (from 1756)**

- **FamilySearch collection:** *Louisiana, Probate Records, 1756–1984* (collection 2078341) — **~457,000 indexed records with images, free.** The 1756–1769 portion covers the final French colonial years.
- Original estate files also at the **Louisiana Historical Center** and **New Orleans Notarial Archives**

---

### 7. Military Records
**Digitization: Largely physical only (France)**

Louisiana was garrisoned by the *Troupes de la Marine* and *Compagnies franches de la Marine* throughout the French period.

- **Record types:** Muster rolls (*revues*), service records, officer commissions, militia records
- **Where held:**
  - **Service Historique de la Défense (SHD)**, Vincennes, France — primary French military archive; in-person or written request
  - **ANOM** — colonial correspondence sometimes names officers and soldiers
  - **Louisiana Historical Center** — some militia records
- Many soldiers appear in parish registers and notarial acts even when formal service records are elusive

---

### 8. Immigration / Arrival Records
**Digitization: Published finding aids (print/online); voyage database indexed online (free)**

Several well-documented immigration waves:

| Wave | Period | Key Source |
|---|---|---|
| Company of the West ships | 1718–1721 | Published emigrant lists; *New Orleans Genesis* |
| German Coast settlers | ~1720s | Published Creole genealogies; notarial acts |
| Acadian exiles (*Cajuns*) | 1755–1785 | Arsenault, *Généalogie des familles acadiennes*; Brasseaux, *The Founding of New Acadia* |
| Enslaved Africans (ongoing) | Throughout | ***Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database*** — slavevoyages.org (free); voyage-level data by ship, date, and African port of origin |

The *Slavevoyages.org* database is indispensable for tracing African-descended ancestors — it documents individual voyages to Louisiana with origin ports, dates, and vessel names.

---

### 9. Cemetery Records
**Digitization: Browse (Find A Grave); burial data primarily in parish registers**

Formal cemeteries in New Orleans date from 1789 (St. Louis Cemetery No. 1) — after the French colonial period. For the 1700s, **burial information comes almost entirely from Catholic parish registers**, not surviving headstones. Use FamilySearch collection 1612905 for burial entries.

---

## Repository guide

### Online

| Repository | Holdings for this period | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch.org** | *Church Records 1718–1925* (~988K); *Probate Records 1756–1984* (~457K) | Free |
| **ANOM** (anom.archivesnationales.culture.gouv.fr) | Colonial censuses, concession files, admin correspondence | Free catalog; partial digitization |
| **Louisiana State Land Office** (doa.la.gov/OLO) | Colonial land grant confirmations | Free |
| **HathiTrust / Google Books** | *American State Papers: Public Lands* | Free |
| **Slavevoyages.org** | Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database | Free |
| **notarialarchives.org** | Select notarial acts and indexes | Free (partial) |
| **Gallica** (gallica.bnf.fr) | Colonial maps, printed sources, some manuscripts | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | Some overlapping Louisiana church/vital collections | Subscription |

### Physical

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Louisiana Historical Center** (Louisiana State Museum), New Orleans | Superior Council records; Cabildo records | In-person; contact for appointments |
| **New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center**, New Orleans | Notarial acts from 1730s | In-person; some online |
| **Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives**, New Orleans | Original parish registers | In-person; appointment required |
| **Earl K. Long Library (UNO)**, New Orleans | Colonial register microfilm; Louisiana Collection | In-person |
| **Louisiana State Archives**, Baton Rouge | State-era + some colonial materials | In-person; limited remote |
| **ANOM**, Aix-en-Provence, France | Full colonial administrative archive | In-person; online catalog |
| **Service Historique de la Défense (SHD)**, Vincennes, France | French military service records, muster rolls | In-person or written request |

---

## Records NOT online

- **Superior Council records** (Louisiana Historical Center) — bulk of colonial court, contract, and estate records; partially microfilmed but mostly requires in-person research
- **ANOM censuses and correspondence** — catalog online, but most documents require ordering reproductions or visiting Aix-en-Provence
- **French military muster rolls** (SHD, Vincennes) — not digitized
- **Notarial acts pre-1800** — many indexed but not digitized; in-person visit or local researcher required
- **Rural *poste* registers** outside New Orleans — on FamilySearch microfilm but may not be fully indexed

---

## Known record losses

- **New Orleans fires of 1788 and 1794** — Destroyed large portions of the French Quarter. St. Louis Cathedral registers survived (stored separately) but with some gaps.
- **Scattered early post registers** — Biloxi and Mobile-area records, predating New Orleans, are fragmentary; some transferred to Alabama/Mississippi repositories after boundary changes.
- **ANOM document losses** — Some colonial correspondence was lost or damaged in transit over the centuries.
- **Substitutes:** Superior Council estate inventories, notarial acts, and published genealogical compilations (*New Orleans Genesis*, Winston De Ville's transcriptions) partially compensate for register gaps.

---

## Research tips

1. **Search FamilySearch first.** *Louisiana, Church Records, 1718–1925* (collection 1612905) is indexed and free — start here for any family present in the colony. Try spelling variants; 18th-century French clerks spelled phonetically and inconsistently.

2. **Prioritize the marriage act.** French Catholic marriage entries name both sets of parents, their status (living or deceased), and often their origins — far more informative than contemporaneous Anglo-American records. Finding the marriage is often the key to a generation breakthrough.

3. **Learn key French genealogical terms.** *parrain/marraine* (godfather/godmother) · *témoins* (witnesses) · *feu* (the late, as in *feu Jean Dupont*) · *légitime* (legitimate child) · *veuve* (widow) · *contrat de mariage* (marriage contract)

4. **Use *Code Noir* entries for enslaved ancestors.** Baptismal registers record enslaved persons by name, godparents, the enslaver's name, and occasionally African ethnic origins. Estate inventories name enslaved individuals with ages and skills. These are often the **only** primary sources for enslaved ancestry in this period.

5. **Track witnesses and godparents.** Chain migration was the norm — families from the same French or German village settled near one another. Witnesses and godparents in parish registers were typically kin or close neighbors; tracking these networks reveals collateral relatives and potential places of origin.

6. **Check the American State Papers for land data.** After the 1803 Louisiana Purchase, all colonial land claimants had to re-petition Congress. The *Public Lands* series contains thousands of Louisiana petitions naming original grantees going back generations — available free on HathiTrust.

7. **Acadian families: use published genealogies.** If your family arrived 1755–1785, they may be Acadian exiles. Arsenault's *Généalogie des familles acadiennes* and White's *Dictionnaire généalogique des familles acadiennes* trace these families back to Nova Scotia registers from the 1630s.

8. **Colonial districts ≠ modern parishes.** Search by the historical *poste* (Pointe Coupée, Natchitoches, Attakapas, etc.), not by modern parish name, to locate the right registers and Superior Council records.

9. **For New Orleans families, use the Notarial Archives.** Marriage contracts in the notarial acts often survive gaps in parish registers and contain even richer family detail.

10. **Consider hiring a local researcher** for Superior Council and Notarial Archives work — both require in-person access in New Orleans. The **Genealogical Research Society of New Orleans (GRSNO)** and the **Association of Professional Genealogists** can provide referrals.

---

*Guide saved to `louisiana-french-colonial-locality-guide.md` · Compiled June 2026 · Sources: FamilySearch Wiki "Louisiana Genealogy" and "Louisiana Land and Property"; Wikipedia "French Louisiana"; FamilySearch collections 1612905, 2078341.*

---

# Locality Guide: Pennsylvania Anthracite Coal Region (1860–1910)
**Focus counties: Schuylkill · Luzerne · Lackawanna · Carbon · Northumberland**

---

## The single most important fact for this research period

**Pennsylvania had no statewide vital registration before 1906.** There are no state-issued birth or death certificates for the entire late-1800s coal mining era. **Church registers are your primary source for births and deaths.** Marriage records are the only civil vital records available at the county level before 1906.

---

## Jurisdiction overview

The anthracite coal belt occupies five counties in northeastern Pennsylvania. Coal mining expanded dramatically from the 1820s–1870s, drawing Irish, Welsh, and German immigrants first, then a massive wave of Polish, Lithuanian, Slovak, Ukrainian, and Italian miners from the 1880s onward. Company-owned "patch towns" housed workers in rented company housing — meaning many miners appear in *no* land or deed records. Key boundary note: **Lackawanna County was carved from Luzerne County in 1878** — all records for Scranton-area families before that date are in Luzerne.

---

## Record types — by priority for the late 1800s

### 1. ⛪ Church records — MOST CRITICAL
The substitute for missing civil vital registration. Ethnic parishes were organized **by nationality**, not geography — an Irish Catholic family and a Polish Catholic family in the same block attended completely different parishes with separate registers.

| Denomination | Community | Language | Held at |
|---|---|---|---|
| Roman Catholic (Irish) | All coal counties | English | Diocese of Allentown / Scranton archives |
| Roman Catholic (Polish, Lithuanian, Slovak) | Shenandoah, Nanticoke, Hazleton, Pittston | Polish / Lithuanian / Slovak / Latin | Diocesan archives |
| Welsh Congregational / Calvinist Methodist | Schuylkill, Carbon, Luzerne | Welsh / English | Welsh-American Heritage Museum; individual chapels |
| Lutheran (German) | Schuylkill early settlers | German / English | Lutheran Archives Center, Philadelphia |
| Greek / Byzantine Catholic | Carpatho-Rusyn, Ukrainian miners | Church Slavonic | Parish archives; Byzantine Catholic Diocese |

**FamilySearch:** *Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985* — 1.2 million records, indexed + images ([search](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1401638)). Not comprehensive — many ethnic parishes aren't yet digitized.

---

### 2. 📋 Federal census records

| Year | Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 1860, 1870, 1880 | Indexed + images | 1880 is the most detailed for family structure |
| **1890** | **Destroyed (1921 fire)** | ~99% lost nationally — plan for this gap |
| 1900, 1910 | Indexed + images | 1900 lists years in U.S. and citizenship; essential for immigrant miners |

Pennsylvania had **no state census**. Mortality schedules (1850–1885) capture mine accident deaths.

---

### 3. ⛏️ Occupational records — unique to coal mining

| Record | County | Digitization | Access |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mine Inspector Annual Reports** (1870+) | All coal counties | Microfilm / physical | PA State Archives, Harrisburg |
| **Mine Worker Employment Cards** (1870–1900) | Schuylkill | **95% indexed + images** on FamilySearch | Free — searchable by name |
| **Mine Accident Reports** (1870–1895) | Luzerne | Browse-only images on FamilySearch | Browse image by image |
| **Colliery Payroll Ledgers** (1878–1900) | Lackawanna | Browse-only images on FamilySearch | Browse image by image |
| **Company employment records** | All counties | Physical only | PA State Archives; Historical Society of Schuylkill County |
| **UMWA union records** (1890+) | All counties | Physical only | IUP Archives, Indiana, PA |

The **Mine Inspector Annual Reports** are a hidden gem — each fatal accident lists the miner's name, age, nationality, and colliery.

---

### 4. 🛂 Naturalization records — CRITICAL for immigrants

Pre-1906, miners could naturalize in **any court of record**. Records are at the county courthouse and Pennsylvania State Archives. Schuylkill County naturalization records (1840s–1906) are at the **PA State Archives, Harrisburg**. Naturalization papers typically name the exact birthplace, arrival date and port, and witness names — among the most genealogically rich records available. Post-1906: NARA (federal courts only).

---

### 5. 🚢 Passenger / immigration records

- **Ports:** New York (Castle Garden 1855–1890; Ellis Island 1892+) and Philadelphia
- **Post-1895 manifests** are far richer — name destination contact and home-country relative (often the key to finding the origin village)
- **Ellis Island database:** free at libertyellisfoundation.org | **Ancestry:** subscription, full manifests indexed
- Always **read the full manifest page** — chain-migration companions from the same village are often listed in adjacent rows

---

### 6. 💍 Marriage records

- County-level from colonial period — licenses and bonds at county courthouse
- **FamilySearch:** *Pennsylvania, County Marriages, 1885–1950* — 524K records, indexed + images ([search](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1921317))

---

### 7. 📰 Newspapers

The ***Miners' Journal*** (Pottsville, 1825–present) is indispensable — obituaries routinely named the deceased's Irish townland, Polish village, or Slovak county of origin. Available on **Newspapers.com** (subscription) and at the **Historical Society of Schuylkill County** on microfilm. Other key papers: *Wilkes-Barre Times*, *Scranton Tribune*, *Shenandoah Herald*.

---

### 8. ⚖️ Probate & court records

- *Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994* — 893K records, indexed + images ([search](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1417683))
- Orphans' Court guardianship files (when a miner died young): physical at county courthouses
- Molly Maguires trial records (Schuylkill County, 1875–1876): Historical Society of Schuylkill County

---

### 9. 🏦 Death Certificates (post-1906 only)

*Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906–1968* — 4.2 million records, indexed + images ([search](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2255317)). Though just outside the late 1800s window, these capture deaths of miners born in the 1840s–1880s and often list birthplace and parents' names.

---

## Repository quick reference

| Repository | What they hold |
|---|---|
| **PA State Archives**, Harrisburg | Vital records (1906+); naturalization records; Mine Inspector reports; company employment records; land records |
| **Historical Society of Schuylkill County**, Pottsville | *Miners' Journal* microfilm; local histories; mining company records; church records |
| **Anthracite Heritage Museum**, Scranton | Mining community records; ethnic organization records; photographs |
| **Diocese of Allentown / Scranton Archives** | Catholic parish registers for all coal counties |
| **NARA Mid-Atlantic Region**, Philadelphia | Philadelphia passenger lists; federal court naturalizations; Civil War records |
| **IUP Archives**, Indiana, PA | UMWA union membership rolls (1890+) |
| **County courthouses** | Deed books; probate files; marriage licenses; court records |

---

## Top 5 research tips

1. **Identify the ethnic parish first** — it holds birth and death records your family will appear in nowhere else before 1906.
2. **Plan for the missing 1890 census** — use 1880 + 1900 on either side, plus church registers, newspapers, and tax assessment lists to fill the gap decade.
3. **Naturalization papers reveal the origin village** — check Schuylkill County Common Pleas Court naturalizations at the PA State Archives before assuming an immigrant's origin is unknown.
4. **Read full passenger manifest pages** — the contact name in America and relative's name in Europe are often the only surviving link to the home village.
5. **Mine Inspector Annual Reports (1870+) capture accident deaths** — if your ancestor died in the mines before civil registration began, this may be the only official record of their death.
