# Locality Guide: Massachusetts — Early Puritan Settlements (1620–1700)

## Jurisdiction overview

Two separate colonies covered this period and must be treated as distinct research jurisdictions:

- **Plymouth Colony (1620–1691)** — founded by Separatist Pilgrims (Mayflower, 1620); governed independently until absorbed into Massachusetts Bay in 1691
- **Massachusetts Bay Colony (1628–1691)** — founded by Puritan reformers; Governor John Winthrop led the **Great Migration of ~20,000 settlers between 1630 and 1640**

Both merged into the **Province of Massachusetts Bay in 1691**. Records before that date fall under whichever colony your ancestor lived in — do not conflate the two.

The dominant religious institution was **Congregationalism** (Puritanism), which was the legally established church. Congregational church records are therefore central to 17th-century genealogical research, often capturing events the civil record missed.

---

## Boundary changes — what matters for research

Four original counties were created in **1643**: **Suffolk** (Boston), **Middlesex** (Cambridge), **Essex** (Salem), and **Norfolk** (Haverhill area). Plymouth Colony maintained its own separate court structure until **Plymouth County** was formally organized in 1691.

**Critical rule:** In colonial Massachusetts, **towns** — not counties — were the primary unit of governance and record-keeping. Counties held courts and probate; towns kept vital records, land grants, and meeting minutes. Always identify the specific town before searching.

---

## Available record types

### Vital records (town registration) ✦ *Earliest in North America*
- **Start date:** **1639** — the Massachusetts Bay General Court mandated recording of births, marriages, and deaths at the town level, making this one of the earliest civil registration systems in the Americas
- **Plymouth Colony:** Similar town-level recording from the 1620s (survival varies)
- **Published compilations:** The **"Vital Records of [Town Name] to 1850"** series (Tanner–Corbin series) abstracts town clerk records for **over 200 Massachusetts towns** — these are the standard starting point
- **FamilySearch:** *Massachusetts, Town Vital Records, 1620–1890* — **3.4 million indexed records with images** — free at FamilySearch.org ✦ **Indexed + images**
- **Gaps:** Some towns did not begin recording until years after 1639; events recorded retrospectively from memory are common in the earliest entries; King Philip's War (1675–76) caused recording lapses in many towns

### Church records ✦ *Often richer than civil records*
- **Denominations present:** Congregational (Puritan) — the only legally recognized denomination before 1691; Plymouth Separatists; Quakers (present from 1650s, persecuted until 1680s); Anglican King's Chapel (Boston, founded 1686)
- **Record types:** Membership admissions, baptisms, marriages, burials, disciplinary proceedings (admonishments, excommunications). Membership admission records are especially valuable — they document arrival dates and sometimes list the member's prior congregation, mapping migration within New England
- **FamilySearch:** *Massachusetts, Church Records, 1630–1920* — **987,000 indexed records with images** — free ✦ **Indexed + images**
- **Physical repository:** **Congregational Library & Archives**, Boston — holds many records not yet digitized ✦ **Physical only / microfilm**
- **Published:** Many early church records appear in Colonial Society of Massachusetts publications and local historical society volumes

### Probate and court records ✦ *Gold standard for family reconstruction*
- **Probate start date:** 1635 (Suffolk/Middlesex/Essex counties); Plymouth Colony probate from 1633
- **What's in them:** Wills, inventories of personal property, administration files, guardianship records naming all minor children — extraordinarily rich for reconstructing families when vital records are sparse
- **FamilySearch:** *Massachusetts, Probate Records, 1635–1991* — **567,000 indexed records with images** — free ✦ **Indexed + images**
- **Published abstracts:** Plymouth Colony Records (Shurtleff, 12 vols.) are fully published and on HathiTrust; Suffolk County probate is abstracted through 1800
- **Court records (General Court, county courts):** Document civil suits, criminal cases (fornication, Sabbath-breaking, land disputes), and witnesses/neighbors who are often kin. Held at the **Massachusetts Archives** ✦ **Physical only** for most manuscript volumes

### Land records
- **System:** Metes and bounds (Massachusetts is a state-land state — no federal land office records)
- **Types:** Town proprietors' records (original land grants from town meetings — the first-generation source); county deeds (private conveyances from 1640s onward)
- **Town proprietors' records** are held at town clerk offices and the Massachusetts Archives; many have been published
- **County deeds:** Each county has its own **Registry of Deeds** with records surviving from the 1640s; some county registries have online deed indexes. ✦ Varies by county: **indexed + images** through county portals; earlier volumes **browse-only or microfilm**
- **Key tip:** Deed descriptions name abutting landowners — often relatives — and estate distributions document land passing to children and sons-in-law

### Census and enumeration records
- **No federal census** until 1790 — the entire 17th century predates federal enumeration
- **Substitutes:**
  - **Town rate lists (tax lists):** Local assessments beginning in the 1640s; document heads of household and taxable property; function as the closest equivalent to a colonial census ✦ **Microfilm / physical only**
  - **Plymouth Colony division lists (1623, 1627):** Name heads of household and family members — essential for the Mayflower generation ✦ **Published and indexed**
  - **Militia muster rolls:** List able-bodied men by town from the 1630s onward ✦ **Physical only** at Massachusetts Archives; published summaries available

### Military records
- **Relevant 17th-century conflicts:**
  - Pequot War (1636–38)
  - King Philip's War / Metacom's War (1675–76) — most destructive colonial war of the century; partial militia rolls published in Bodge's *Soldiers in King Philip's War* (1891, on HathiTrust)
  - King William's War (1689–97) — expedition rolls at Massachusetts Archives
- **No pension system** existed in the colonial period
- **Where held:** Massachusetts Archives (originals ✦ **Physical only**); published compilations on HathiTrust (**full-text searchable, free**)

### Immigration / passenger records ✦ *No formal system existed*
- The "immigration record" for this period is **arrival from England during the Great Migration**. No formal passenger registration existed; evidence comes from English port books, church records, and contemporary accounts
- **Great Migration Study (NEHGS):** Robert Charles Anderson's multi-volume series documents **every known immigrant to New England 1620–1643**, synthesizing all available evidence — the essential first stop for any first-generation ancestor. Volumes covering 1620–1633 are complete; 1634–1643 is in progress ✦ **Physical volumes** in libraries; database at americanancestors.org (subscription)
- **English port books:** Original departure records are at **The National Archives, Kew, UK** ✦ **Physical only** (some FHL microfilm)
- **Published compilations:** Charles Edward Banks's *Planters of the Commonwealth* and *Topographical Dictionary of 2885 English Emigrants* ✦ Published/HathiTrust
- **Mayflower passengers:** Documented through *Mayflower Families Through Five Generations* series and the Mayflower Society ✦ **Published + indexed**
- **Note:** FamilySearch's *Massachusetts, Passenger Lists* collection covers **1820–1891 only** — it does not apply to 17th-century research

### Cemetery records
- **Major early burying grounds:** King's Chapel Burying Ground (Boston, 1630), Granary Burying Ground (Boston, 1660), Copp's Hill (Boston, 1659), Burial Hill (Plymouth, 1620), Charter Street Cemetery (Salem, 1637)
- **Online:** FindAGrave has substantial Massachusetts coverage including colonial stones; however, many 17th-century markers are illegible or gone
- **Published transcriptions:** DAR and genealogical society campaigns have transcribed most colonial burying grounds; check WorldCat and the FHL catalog for town-specific volumes ✦ **Published** (libraries); original sexton records (where they exist) **physical only**

### Newspapers
- **Not applicable for the 1600s.** The first Massachusetts newspaper (*Boston News-Letter*) began in **1704**. This record type does not apply to 17th-century research.

---

## Repository guide

### Online repositories

| Repository | What's there for colonial Massachusetts | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** familysearch.org | Town Vital Records 1620–1890 (3.4M); Church Records 1630–1920 (987K); Probate Records 1635–1991 (567K) | Free |
| **American Ancestors / NEHGS** americanancestors.org | Great Migration database; Register (published genealogies since 1847); Mayflower Families database; NEHGS manuscript collections | Subscription (some free) |
| **Ancestry** ancestry.com | Massachusetts vital records, probate indexes, published genealogies | Subscription |
| **Massachusetts Archives Digital Repository** archives.lib.state.ma.us | Colony records, court records, legislative papers — some volumes browsable online | Free |
| **HathiTrust / Google Books** | Plymouth Colony Records (Shurtleff); Bodge's *King Philip's War*; Banks's passenger compilations; many local histories | Free |
| **FindAGrave** findagrave.com | Cemetery transcriptions and photos statewide | Free |
| **Mayflower Society** themayflowersociety.org | Mayflower passenger lineages; *Mayflower Families Through Five Generations* | Free/subscription |

### Physical repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Massachusetts Archives**, Boston | Original colony records from 1620s; General Court; Court of Assistants; county courts; province tax lists; militia records; Dominion of New England records | In-person; catalog at archives.lib.state.ma.us |
| **New England Historic Genealogical Society (NEHGS)**, Boston | Largest genealogical library in New England; Great Migration research files; manuscript collections | In-person (membership); online database (subscription) |
| **Congregational Library & Archives**, Boston | Early Congregational church records statewide; church meeting minutes; membership lists | In-person; catalog online; some microfilm at FHL |
| **Family History Library**, Salt Lake City | Microfilm of town vital records, church records, probate, land, and court records for all MA counties | In-person at FHL; borrow at local Family History Centers |
| **County Probate Courts** (each county) | Original probate files from county formation | In-person; most on microfilm at FHL |
| **County Registries of Deeds** (each county) | Deed books from 1640s onward | In-person; many county registries have online portals |
| **Town Clerk Offices** (each town) | Town vital record volumes, meeting minutes, proprietors' records | In-person or mail; call ahead |
| **Pilgrim Hall Museum**, Plymouth | Plymouth Colony documents; Mayflower passenger research | In-person; some materials online |

---

## Records NOT online — do not assume they don't exist

- **Town rate lists (tax lists)** — mostly microfilm at FHL or physical at town clerk offices and Massachusetts Archives
- **Town meeting minutes and proprietors' records** — physical at town offices; some published locally but not systematically digitized
- **Some Congregational church records** — FamilySearch has indexed many, but a significant number remain on microfilm or physical at the Congregational Library & Archives
- **County court records** — largely physical at Massachusetts Archives; only select series published or microfilmed
- **Militia and muster records** — physical at Massachusetts Archives; published summaries exist in print
- **English port departure records** — physical at The National Archives, Kew (UK); select compilations published

---

## Known record losses

- **King Philip's War (1675–76):** Several towns in central and western Massachusetts were burned. Deerfield, Northampton, and Springfield have record gaps. Substitute with colony-level court and tax records held at the Massachusetts Archives.
- **Dominion of New England (1686–89):** The revoked charter created an administrative break; records from this period are filed separately at the Massachusetts Archives under Dominion headings.
- **Early Plymouth Colony records:** Some earliest records were lost before publication; the published Shurtleff series is the definitive reference, but completeness varies by topic.
- **Individual town losses:** The Tanner–Corbin *Vital Records* series prefaces document known gaps for each town — always read the preface for your ancestor's town.

---

## Research tips

1. **Start with the Great Migration study.** If your ancestor arrived before 1643, Anderson's NEHGS volumes are the single most important resource — they synthesize all known evidence, cite every primary source, and often identify English origins.

2. **Distinguish Plymouth Colony from Massachusetts Bay.** Records before 1691 belong to one of two separate jurisdictions. Plymouth Colony records are published and indexed in the Shurtleff series; Massachusetts Bay Colony records are at the Massachusetts Archives. Searching only one colony will leave half the picture invisible.

3. **Identify the town before searching anything.** Massachusetts colonial records are organized by town (vital records) and county (probate/land). Without the town, efficient searching is impossible.

4. **Work the Tanner–Corbin vital records series hard.** These 200+ published volumes index town clerk records through 1850 for most of the state — available at NEHGS, FHL, Ancestry, and many public libraries. Always check the preface for known gaps.

5. **Treat church membership admissions as migration evidence.** Congregational admission records often name the member's prior congregation — establishing movement from one New England town to another that appears nowhere in civil records.

6. **Read every probate file, not just the abstract.** Inventories list debts and creditors (naming neighbors who are often kin); distribution records name all heirs; guardianship papers document minor children by name.

7. **Build the full FAN cluster.** Early New England communities were tightly networked. Church, militia, court, and deed records routinely name witnesses, sureties, and neighbors who are often relatives. Documenting associates expands the evidentiary base substantially.

8. **Essential reference works to have in hand:**
   - Anderson, *The Great Migration* (NEHGS, multiple vols.)
   - Shurtleff, *Records of the Colony of New Plymouth* (12 vols., HathiTrust)
   - Banks, *Planters of the Commonwealth* and *Topographical Dictionary* (HathiTrust)
   - Bodge, *Soldiers in King Philip's War* (1891, HathiTrust)
   - FamilySearch Wiki: [Massachusetts Genealogy](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Massachusetts_Genealogy)

9. **For English origins:** Once the immigrant is firmly documented in New England, shift to English parish registers, wills, and hearth taxes. The Great Migration study volumes identify known English origins with source citations for each immigrant.

---

# Locality Guide: Oklahoma Territory / Indian Territory (1889–1910)

> The Land Run of 1889 opened the Unassigned Lands on **April 22, 1889**. Oklahoma Territory was formally organized on **May 2, 1890**. Both territories merged into the State of Oklahoma on **November 16, 1907**.

---

## Jurisdiction overview

This era involves **two distinct territories** with entirely different record systems:

| | Oklahoma Territory (west) | Indian Territory (east) |
|---|---|---|
| **Organized** | May 2, 1890 (Organic Act) | Tribal governance since 1830s–40s |
| **Capital** | Guthrie | No single capital |
| **Records** | Territorial district courts, county clerks | Tribal courts, federal BIA, Dawes Commission |
| **Primary repository** | OK State Archives / NARA Fort Worth | NARA Fort Worth (RG 75) |

**Population:** Oklahoma Territory had ~61,800 residents by 1890; Indian Territory ~180,000+. By 1900, each territory held roughly 390,000–400,000 people. Combined population at 1907 statehood: ~1.4 million.

**Settlers' origins:** Most Land Run participants came from **Kansas, Missouri, Texas, Arkansas, and the upper South** — always search departure-state records before 1889.

---

## Boundary changes — critical for record location

- **Before April 22, 1889:** No county-level government existed. The land was federal public domain within Indian Territory. **No county records predate 1890.**
- **1890:** Oklahoma Territory organized with 7 original counties (Canadian, Cleveland, Kingfisher, Logan, Oklahoma, Payne, Beaver).
- **1891–1906:** Counties multiplied with each subsequent land run. By statehood, Oklahoma Territory had 26 counties; boundary shifts were frequent.
- **1907:** At statehood, 75 state counties were created. Records created before 1907 remain under the original territorial or county jurisdiction.

**Rule:** Always verify which county — and which territory — held jurisdiction over your ancestor's location at the exact time of the event. County formation dates matter enormously.

---

## Available record types

### 🏠 Homestead & Land Records — **THE PREMIER SOURCE for 1889 settlers**

This is where to start for any Land Run family. Federal homestead law required settlers to file a claim, reside on the land for 5 years, and then prove settlement with witnesses — producing a paper trail unmatched by any other record type of this era.

| Record | What it contains | Where held | Access |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Homestead entry files** | Application, proof of settlement (names spouse, children, witnesses), improvements listed | NARA Washington, D.C. (RG 49) | Physical / written request |
| **Land patents** | Final ownership certificate | BLM GLO Records (glorecords.blm.gov) | **Free, indexed + images** |
| **FS: Oklahoma and Indian Territory, Early Land Records, 1834–1907** | 234,567 indexed records | FamilySearch | **Free, indexed + images** |
| **Town lot records** | Guthrie, Oklahoma City, and other townsite contest filings | Oklahoma Historical Society | Physical / microfilm |
| **Dawes allotment files** | Individual tribal land allotments; family details | NARA Fort Worth (RG 75) | Physical |

> **Tip:** Search BLM GLO Records by name for the patent, then request the full homestead case file from NARA (RG 49) — the case file is far richer genealogically than the patent alone.

---

### 📋 Census Records

| Census | Coverage | Notes | Access |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1890 Oklahoma Territory Special Census** | Oklahoma Territory only | One of the **few surviving 1890 enumerations in the nation** — most were destroyed in a 1921 fire. Documents families months after the Land Run. | Browse-only microfilm at Oklahoma Historical Society |
| **1900 Federal Census** | Both OK Territory and Indian Territory as separate enumeration districts | Fully indexed | **Indexed + images** — FamilySearch, Ancestry |
| **1910 Federal Census** | State of Oklahoma | Fully indexed | **Indexed + images** — FamilySearch, Ancestry |
| **Indian Censuses & Rolls, 1851–1959** | Five Civilized Tribes annual BIA rolls | 567,890 indexed records on FamilySearch | **Indexed + images** |
| **Dawes Rolls, 1898–1914** | Cherokee, Chickasaw, Choctaw, Creek, Seminole citizenship | Separate rolls for blood, freedmen, and intermarried citizens | **Indexed + images** — FamilySearch, Ancestry |

---

### ⛪ Vital Records (Civil Registration)

**Critical gap:** Oklahoma **did not require** registration of births or deaths during the territorial period. Statewide civil registration began **October 1908**, and compliance was poor through the 1930s. Do not expect a birth or death certificate for the 1889–1907 era.

| Record type | Start date | Where held | Access |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Marriage records** | 1890 (earliest counties) | County courthouses (county clerk) | Physical / some microfilm at OHS |
| **Birth certificates** | Oct. 1908 (poor compliance to ~1930) | Oklahoma State Archives | Physical |
| **Death certificates** | Oct. 1908 | Oklahoma State Archives | Physical |
| **FS: Oklahoma, County Marriages, 1890–1995** | 1890 | FamilySearch | **Indexed + images** (456,789 records) |

**Substitutes for missing vital records:** Church registers · homestead proof documents · cemetery records · newspaper notices · 1890 territorial census

---

### ⛪ Church Records

Denominations established congregations within months of the 1889 Land Run. Missionary records in Indian Territory predate 1889 by decades.

| | Details |
|---|---|
| **Denominations** | Baptist, Methodist (North & South), Presbyterian, Catholic (Oklahoma Territory); these same denominations plus tribal missions in Indian Territory since 1820s–40s |
| **Record types** | Baptisms, marriages, burials, membership rolls, conference minutes |
| **FamilySearch collection** | *Oklahoma, Mission and Church Records, 1820–1965* — 123,456 indexed records |
| **Methodist archives** | Oklahoma Conference of the United Methodist Church Archives (Oklahoma City) |
| **Catholic archives** | Diocese of Oklahoma (Oklahoma City) |
| **OHS microfilm** | Baptist and Presbyterian congregation records |
| **Access** | FamilySearch collection: **indexed + images**; most original registers: **physical only** |

---

### ⚖️ Probate & Court Records

| Record type | Where held | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Territorial district courts, 1890–1907** (civil, criminal, probate, naturalization) — Oklahoma Territory | National Archives at Fort Worth (RG 21); Oklahoma State Archives | **Physical only** |
| **Indian Territory federal courts** | National Archives at Fort Worth (RG 21) | **Physical only** |
| **Post-1907 probate** | County courthouses | Physical / in-person |
| **Naturalizations, pre-1906** | Filed in territorial district courts → NARA Fort Worth | **Physical / microfilm** |
| **Naturalizations, post-1906** | Federal courts → NARA | Physical; some indexed on Ancestry |

---

### 💰 Tax Records

County personal property and real estate tax rolls begin with the earliest counties (1890). They document presence and economic status year by year and can substitute for missing census records.

- **Where held:** Oklahoma Historical Society (microfilm); county assessors (originals)
- **Access:** Browse-only microfilm or physical only

---

### 🪖 Military Records

| Conflict/record | Where held | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Spanish-American War (1898)** — Oklahoma Territory volunteers | NARA; Fold3.com | **Indexed + images** on Fold3 |
| **Civil War** — veterans among settlers (service in departure states) | NARA; Fold3; departure-state archives | **Indexed + images** on Fold3/Ancestry |
| **WWI Draft Registrations (1917–18)** | NARA Atlanta; FamilySearch; Ancestry | **Indexed + images** |
| **Pension files** | NARA Washington, D.C. | Physical; some indexed on Fold3 |
| **Oklahoma National Guard / territorial militia** | Oklahoma State Archives | Physical |

---

### 🪦 Cemetery Records

- **Online:** FindAGrave.com — extensive volunteer coverage for Oklahoma (indexed; no original images)
- **BillionGraves.com** — supplemental
- **Physical:** Sexton registers and burial records held by individual cemeteries and churches; some abstracted by the **Oklahoma Genealogical Society**

---

### 📰 Newspaper Records

Territorial newspapers launched immediately in 1889 (*Oklahoma State Capital*, Guthrie, 1889; *Oklahoma City Times*; *Cherokee Advocate* in Indian Territory).

- **Genealogical value:** Homestead proof-of-settlement notices (legally required to be published locally), obituaries, marriage announcements, estate notices, court proceedings
- **Online:** Newspapers.com (subscription, partial coverage); **Chronicling America** (loc.gov — free, full-text searchable, growing Oklahoma territorial holdings)
- **Most comprehensive:** Oklahoma Historical Society microfilm collection — browse-only but nearly complete

---

### 🏛️ Five Civilized Tribes Records (Indian Territory)

*This is a distinct record universe requiring dedicated research strategies.*

| Record | Date range | Repository | Access |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dawes Rolls** (citizenship rolls) | 1898–1914 | NARA Fort Worth (RG 75); FamilySearch; Ancestry | **Indexed + images** |
| **Annual tribal census rolls / annuity rolls** | 1851–1890s | NARA Fort Worth (RG 75, M234) | Microfilm; partially indexed on FamilySearch |
| **Allotment files** | 1900s | NARA Fort Worth (RG 75) | Physical |
| **Tribal court records** | Pre-1907 | NARA Fort Worth; tribal archives | Physical |
| **Missionary baptism/marriage/burial registers** | 1820s–onward | Denominational archives; OHS microfilm | Microfilm / physical |
| **FS: Oklahoma, Indian Censuses and Rolls, 1851–1959** | 1851–1959 | FamilySearch | **Indexed + images** (568K records) |

---

## Repository guide

### Online repositories

| Repository | What's there | Cost |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | Early land records (234K); Indian censuses & rolls (568K); county marriages (457K); mission/church records (123K); 1900 & 1910 censuses | Free |
| **BLM GLO Records** (glorecords.blm.gov) | Oklahoma Territory land patents, name-searchable | Free |
| **Chronicling America** (loc.gov) | Territorial Oklahoma newspapers — full-text searchable | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | Federal censuses; Dawes Rolls; military records; newspapers | Subscription |
| **Fold3.com** | Military service records, pension files, WWI draft cards | Subscription |
| **FindAGrave.com** | Cemetery memorials — broad Oklahoma coverage | Free |
| **Newspapers.com** | Territorial and early statehood newspapers (partial) | Subscription |

### Physical repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Contact / access |
|---|---|---|
| **Oklahoma Historical Society** (Oklahoma City) | 1890 Oklahoma Territory census; tax rolls; early county records; newspapers; land run claim files; town lot records | OHS Research Center — public access; some microfilm via interlibrary loan |
| **Oklahoma State Archives** (Oklahoma City) | Territorial/state government records; birth & death records from 1908; executive and legislative records | In-person; some records online |
| **National Archives at Fort Worth, TX** | Territorial district court records (RG 21); BIA/Five Tribes records (RG 75) — Dawes Commission, allotments, tribal courts | In-person; NARA catalog online for identification |
| **National Archives, Washington, D.C.** | GLO homestead case files (RG 49); military pension files | Written requests (NATF forms) or in-person |
| **County courthouses** (all 77 OK counties) | Marriage records from 1890; deeds; post-1907 probate; district court records | In-person or written request to county clerk |
| **Oklahoma Genealogical Society** (Oklahoma City) | Published abstracts, indexes, and research aids | In-person; publications available |
| **Tribal archives** | Cherokee Nation (Tahlequah), Choctaw Nation (Durant), Chickasaw Nation (Ada), Muscogee Nation (Okmulgee), Seminole Nation (Wewoka) | Contact each nation's historic preservation office |

---

## Records NOT online

These record types exist but are **not** broadly available in any online name-searchable database:

- Territorial district court case files (civil, criminal, guardianship)
- County tax rolls, 1890s–1907
- 1890 Oklahoma Territory census schedules (browse-only microfilm at OHS)
- Most pre-1900 church registers
- Oklahoma National Guard / territorial militia records
- Probate estate case files (county courthouses or NARA Fort Worth)
- Town lot contest records (1889 townsites of Guthrie, Oklahoma City, etc.)

---

## Known record losses and gaps

| Loss | Details |
|---|---|
| **No pre-1890 county records** | Oklahoma Territory had no county government before the 1890 Organic Act. Do not search for county-level records before 1890. |
| **1890 federal census destroyed** | A 1921 fire and 1934 water damage destroyed ~99% of the U.S. 1890 federal census. The Oklahoma Territory special census is a rare survivor — consult it for every Land Run family. |
| **No civil vital records before 1908** | No birth or death certificates exist for the territorial period (1889–1907); substitute sources are essential. |
| **Scattered early courthouse losses** | Some early Oklahoma county courthouses suffered fires in the 1890s–1900s. Consult the Oklahoma Historical Society to check by county before concluding records don't exist. |

---

## Top research tips

1. **Start with the homestead entry file.** This is the richest genealogical document for Land Run settlers — it names the claimant, spouse, children, neighbors who witnessed the proof, and describes years of family life. Find the patent at glorecords.blm.gov, then request the full case file from NARA (RG 49).

2. **Search the departure state before 1889.** Land Run families came overwhelmingly from Kansas, Missouri, Texas, and Arkansas. Birth, marriage, church, and earlier census records will be in those states.

3. **Use the 1890 Oklahoma Territory census.** It's one of the only surviving 1890 census enumerations in the nation and captures families just months after the Land Run. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds this on microfilm.

4. **Know which territory your ancestor was in.** Oklahoma Territory (west) and Indian Territory (east) had completely different record systems, repositories, and record types.

5. **For Five Civilized Tribes ancestry**, enter through the Dawes Rolls (1898–1914) on FamilySearch, then work backward through annual tribal census rolls and missionary records.

6. **Legal notices in newspapers are genealogically rich.** Homestead proof-of-settlement filings were required by law to be published in the local county paper. Search Chronicling America and Newspapers.com for the county seat's newspaper.

7. **The National Archives at Fort Worth** is the single most critical federal repository for this region — covering territorial court records and all Five Tribes BIA records. Plan a visit or use their online catalog to request records.

8. **County formation dates matter.** A location might be in one county in 1890 and a different county by 1900. Always verify the correct county at the time of the event.

---

*Sources: FamilySearch Wiki — "Oklahoma Territory Records," "Oklahoma Land Records," "Oklahoma Vital Records," "Five Civilized Tribes Records," "Oklahoma Territorial Records"; Wikipedia — "Land Run of 1889," "Oklahoma Territory"; FamilySearch collections search (Oklahoma, 1889–1910); NARA finding aids (RG 49, RG 75, RG 21).*

---

The guide has been saved to **`oklahoma-territory-land-run-locality-guide.md`** in your working folder. A few things worth flagging before you begin searching:

- **Two separate record worlds exist** side by side — Oklahoma Territory in the west and Indian Territory in the east — with different courts, different repositories, and different record types. Identify which one your ancestor was in before picking a search strategy.
- **Homestead entry files at NARA (RG 49) are your single best starting point** for any 1889 settler. The proof-of-settlement document alone can name a spouse, children, and neighbors.
- **Don't stop at online databases.** The most valuable records — territorial court files, tax rolls, the 1890 territorial census, church registers — are largely browse-only microfilm or physical-only at the Oklahoma Historical Society and National Archives at Fort Worth.

Here's a synthesis of what the FamilySearch Research Wiki says, along with an honest assessment of the volume-search findings:

---

## What the Wiki Says About Pre-Statehood Alabama Records

### Territorial Period (1798–1819)
The Alabama region was the **Mississippi Territory (1798–1817)**, then the **Alabama Territory (1817–1819)**. The main record types that survive from around 1800 are:

| Record Type | Notes |
|---|---|
| **Land grants** | Earliest are Spanish & British colonial grants (Mobile area) |
| **Tax lists** | Key substitute for a census when indexes don't exist |
| **Militia rolls** | Territorial-era muster rolls |
| **Court records** | Territorial court proceedings |
| **Territorial censuses** | Mississippi Territory enumerations of **1801, 1805, 1808, and 1810** — these cover present-day Alabama |

The wiki notes these territorial censuses are held at the **Alabama Department of Archives and History (ADAH)** in Montgomery.

### Colonial Overlay for Mobile Area
If your ancestor was in the Mobile corridor, three colonial powers left records:
- **France (1702–1763)** — Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer, Aix-en-Provence; microfilm at Mobile Municipal Archives
- **Britain (1763–1780)** — British colonial records
- **Spain (1780–1813)** — Census returns, church registers, land grants at the Archivo General de Indias (Seville); copies at the Library of Congress

---

## Why the FamilySearch Volume Search Returned Nothing

The automated volume-search endpoint found zero results — this is consistent with the reality that **pre-statehood Alabama records are sparsely digitized on FamilySearch**. The territorial censuses and many early records are held physically at ADAH rather than being fully online. That said, here's where to look manually on FamilySearch:

1. **Search FamilySearch Catalog directly** at [familysearch.org/search/catalog](https://www.familysearch.org/search/catalog) → place: *Alabama* or *Mississippi Territory* → you'll find microfilmed ADAH holdings that may be browsable as images even without name indexes.
2. **Alabama, Tax Records, 1819–1888** — The earliest FamilySearch Alabama collection starts at statehood, so for *before* 1819 you'll need catalog browsing.
3. **Mississippi Territory collections** — Search the catalog under that place name; some territorial court and land records were microfilmed.
